title: Reliable Recall with Distractions
overview: The ultimate recall test — can {dog_name} come when called even with competing interests like food, other dogs, or exciting smells?
steps: Set up a mild distraction: a treat on the ground under a cup, or a calm friend standing 15 feet away.
Let {dog_name} notice the distraction on the long line.
Call '{dog_name}, come!' in a happy voice. Run backward a few steps.
If they come, throw a MASSIVE party — multiple treats, play, celebration.
If they don't, use the long line to gently guide them. Reward less enthusiastically.
Gradually increase distraction difficulty over weeks.
success_criteria: {dog_name} comes away from a mid-level distraction 7 out of 10 times.
pro_tips: Every successful distraction recall should be the best reward {dog_name} has ever gotten. Make coming to you BETTER than the distraction.
Practice surprise recalls — sometimes the most important recall is when they least expect it.
common_mistakes: Starting with the hardest distraction (other dogs playing) — start with easy ones (interesting smell).
Punishing them for coming late — ALWAYS reward the recall, even if it took 30 seconds.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} consistently chooses the distraction over you, your rewards aren't competing. Try real cooked chicken, a squeaky toy, or chasing you as the reward. Find their currency.

title: Distraction Proofing All Commands
overview: Take every command {dog_name} knows and practice them in increasingly challenging environments. This is what separates a dog who 'knows sit' from one who actually sits when it matters.
steps: List all commands {dog_name} knows: sit, down, stay, come, leave it, place, etc.
Rate each command in 3 environments: home (easy), yard (medium), park (hard).
For each command, practice in progressively harder locations over 2 weeks.
Add common real-world distractions: doorbell, other dogs, food on the ground, guests.
A command isn't 'learned' until it works in 3 different environments at 80%+ reliability.
success_criteria: {dog_name} responds to core commands (sit, down, come, stay, leave it) in 3+ environments at 80%+ reliability.
pro_tips: Each new environment is like starting from scratch for {dog_name}. That's normal. Don't be discouraged.
Bring higher-value treats to harder environments. Environment difficulty = reward upgrade.
common_mistakes: Assuming mastery at home means mastery everywhere — it doesn't.
Getting frustrated when {dog_name} 'forgets' in a new place — they didn't forget, the context changed.
troubleshooting: If a command falls apart in a new environment, go back to the luring/early stages of that command in the new location. Rebuild it quickly — it usually comes back much faster the second time.

title: Go to Bed from Distance
overview: Extend the place command so {dog_name} can be sent to their bed from across the room. Useful for doorbell situations, cooking time, and managing visitors.
steps: Start 2 feet from the bed. Point and say "place." When {dog_name} goes, mark and treat.
Back up to 5 feet. Repeat. Then 10 feet.
Add a dramatic point gesture — arm extended toward the bed.
Practice from different rooms (kitchen to living room bed).
Chain with duration: "place" from distance → stay for 1 minute.
success_criteria: {dog_name} goes to their bed on cue from 15+ feet away and stays for 1 minute.
pro_tips: Place the bed strategically so you can send {dog_name} there during common triggers (doorbell, cooking, eating).
A raised cot is visible from more angles than a flat bed — easier for distance sends.
common_mistakes: Sending from too far before close-range is reliable — distance is the hardest variable.
Not rewarding on the bed — always deliver the treat THERE, not after they come back to you.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} comes to you instead of going to the bed, walk them to the bed together, then gradually increase send distance in small increments.

title: Quiet on Cue (Bark Control)
overview: Rather than trying to stop all barking (which is natural communication), teach {dog_name} a quiet cue that works when you need silence.
steps: Wait for {dog_name} to bark naturally (doorbell, seeing something outside).
Hold a treat at their nose. They will stop barking to sniff. Mark "yes!" and treat.
Repeat 10+ times over multiple barking episodes.
Add the cue "quiet" just before presenting the treat.
Fade the treat: say "quiet," wait for 2 seconds of silence, then treat from your pocket.
success_criteria: {dog_name} stops barking within 3 seconds of the quiet cue 7 out of 10 times.
pro_tips: Acknowledging the bark first ("thank you, I see it") can help some dogs transition to quiet faster.
Some dogs respond better to "enough" or "that'll do" — pick a cue that feels natural.
common_mistakes: Yelling "QUIET!" — your yelling sounds like barking to {dog_name}. They think you are joining in.
Using quiet when {dog_name} is in a full barking frenzy — start when barking is mild.
troubleshooting: If barking is constant and uncontrollable, it may be anxiety or frustration-based rather than alert barking. Identify and address the root cause before training quiet.

title: Teach Spin (Left and Right)
overview: A fun, flashy trick that's easy to teach and great for {dog_name}'s body awareness. Plus it's a crowd-pleaser and confidence builder.
steps: Hold a treat at {dog_name}'s nose level.
Slowly lure their nose in a circle (clockwise first). Their body will follow.
The moment they complete the full circle, mark 'yes!' and treat.
After 5-6 successful lured spins, add the cue 'spin!' just before you lure.
Gradually make the lure motion smaller until just a small finger circle cues the spin.
Teach the opposite direction with a different cue ('twist' or 'other way').
success_criteria: {dog_name} completes a full spin in both directions on verbal cue without a lure.
pro_tips: Some dogs are naturally right-pawed or left-pawed — the easier direction will be obvious quickly.
{breed_tip}
Spin is a great way to redirect excited energy. Ask for a spin instead of jumping.
common_mistakes: Moving the treat too fast — {dog_name} loses the lure. Go slow.
Not teaching both directions — ambidexterity in tricks builds better body awareness.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} only does half a circle and gives up, reward half circles first, then 3/4 circles, then full circles. Shape the behavior in steps.

title: Teach Shake (Paw)
overview: The classic trick that everyone loves. Teach {dog_name} to offer a paw on cue — it's cute, it impresses people, and it practices polite human interaction.
steps: Hold a treat in your closed fist and present it to {dog_name} at chest height.
Most puppies will paw at your fist when they can't lick the treat out. The instant a paw touches your hand, mark 'yes!' and open the fist.
If {dog_name} doesn't paw, gently tickle behind one front paw. When they lift it, mark and treat.
After 5-6 successful reps, add the cue 'shake' or 'paw' just before presenting your fist.
Transition from fist to open palm: present your open hand, say 'shake,' wait for the paw.
Practice with both hands and with different people.
success_criteria: {dog_name} places paw in an open palm on verbal cue 8 out of 10 times.
pro_tips: Don't grab or squeeze {dog_name}'s paw. Let them place it gently. We want willing participation.
{breed_tip}
Shake is a gateway trick — once they know this, 'high five' and 'wave' are easy additions.
common_mistakes: Holding the paw too long — a brief touch is enough. Release before {dog_name} pulls away.
Practicing only with one hand — {dog_name} should shake with either paw to either hand.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} only licks and won't paw, try this: put a piece of tape loosely on their paw. They'll lift it to investigate — mark that lift!

title: Teach Crawl
overview: Teach {dog_name} to army-crawl forward from a down position. This is great for body awareness, core strength, and it's adorable.
steps: Start with {dog_name} in a down position.
Hold a treat at nose level and slowly drag it forward along the floor, away from {dog_name}.
If {dog_name} stays in down and inches forward even slightly, mark 'yes!' and treat.
If they pop up into a stand, reset to down and try again with the treat closer.
Optionally, place a broomstick across two low objects (6 inches high). Lure {dog_name} underneath to encourage staying low.
Build distance: crawl 6 inches, then 1 foot, then 2 feet. Add cue 'crawl' once reliable.
success_criteria: {dog_name} crawls forward 2 feet in a down position on cue.
pro_tips: Practice on carpet or grass — hard floors make crawling uncomfortable for elbows. {breed_tip}
Reward tiny increments at first. Even shifting weight forward counts.
This exercise is a great core workout for {dog_name} — bonus physical benefits.
common_mistakes: Pulling the treat too far forward — {dog_name} stands up because the target is too distant.
Pushing down on {dog_name}'s back to keep them low — this creates resistance, not learning.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} absolutely won't crawl, try under a chair or low table. The physical barrier keeps them low while following the lure.

title: Teach Roll Over
overview: The crowd favorite. Teach {dog_name} to roll from down onto their back and over to the other side. This requires trust, so it's also a great bonding exercise.
steps: Start with {dog_name} in a down on a soft surface (carpet, grass, mat).
Hold a treat at {dog_name}'s nose and slowly lure it toward their shoulder blade.
{dog_name}'s head will follow, and their body should start to roll. The moment they shift onto their side, mark 'yes!' and treat.
Next rep: continue the lure further so they roll onto their back. Mark and treat the back position.
Finally, lure all the way over. Mark and treat the complete roll.
Add verbal cue 'roll over' once {dog_name} completes the full motion with just a small hand gesture.
success_criteria: {dog_name} completes a full roll over on verbal cue 7 out of 10 times.
pro_tips: Most dogs have an easier direction to roll. Go with their natural preference first. {breed_tip}
This is a trust exercise — belly exposure is vulnerable. Keep it fun and low-pressure.
Practice on soft surfaces only. Hard floors discourage rolling.
common_mistakes: Moving the lure too fast — the motion should be smooth and slow.
Forcing a dog who doesn't want to show their belly — never push. Some dogs need more trust-building first.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} won't roll past their side, reward the side position multiple times first. Then ask for a slightly bigger roll each rep. Shape it in stages over 3-5 sessions.

title: Teach 'Find It' (Nose Work Foundation)
overview: Hide treats or toys and let {dog_name} use their nose to find them. This builds confidence, provides mental exercise, and taps into every dog's natural scenting ability.
steps: Show {dog_name} a treat, then toss it 3 feet away. Say 'find it!'
Once {dog_name} understands 'find it' = search for visible treats, hide a treat under one of three cups while they watch.
Say 'find it!' and let them knock over cups to find it. Mark and celebrate!
Hide treats behind the cup without {dog_name} watching. Say 'find it!' and let them use their nose.
Expand to hiding treats around a room: under a shoe, behind a pillow, on a low shelf.
Progress to hiding treats in different rooms. {dog_name} will do room-to-room searches.
success_criteria: {dog_name} finds 3 hidden treats in a room within 2 minutes using their nose.
pro_tips: Use smelly treats — freeze-dried liver, cheese, hot dogs. The smell is the whole game.
{breed_tip}
Nose work is one of the best exercises for rainy days. 10 minutes of scenting = 30 minutes of walking in terms of mental fatigue.
common_mistakes: Hiding treats in the same spots — vary locations so {dog_name} actually has to search.
Helping too quickly. Let {dog_name} problem-solve. It's frustrating to watch but rewarding for them.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} gives up quickly, make the first hides very obvious (treat barely hidden under a napkin). Build difficulty gradually.

title: Teach 'Speak' and 'Quiet'
overview: Putting barking on cue gives you an 'off switch.' When {dog_name} understands 'speak,' you can teach 'quiet' — because now they know what you're asking them to stop.
steps: Identify what makes {dog_name} bark: doorbell, a knock, a squeaky toy, exciting movement.
Trigger a bark, then immediately say 'speak!' and treat. Repeat 10 times. Bark = cue + treat.
Test: say 'speak!' WITHOUT the trigger. If {dog_name} barks, jackpot treat! If not, trigger and retry.
Once 'speak' is reliable, cue one bark, then hold a treat at {dog_name}'s nose and say 'quiet.'
{dog_name} can't bark and sniff simultaneously. The moment they stop barking to sniff, mark 'yes!' and treat.
Practice the chain: 'speak' → one bark → 'quiet' → silence → treat. Gradually extend silence duration.
success_criteria: {dog_name} barks on 'speak' and stops barking on 'quiet' within 3 seconds, 7 out of 10 times.
pro_tips: Don't practice 'speak' if nuisance barking is currently a problem — focus on 'quiet' directly using ex-advanced-004.
{breed_tip}
'Speak' is a great party trick and actually helps with bark management long-term.
common_mistakes: Teaching 'speak' to a dog that already barks too much — only teach this when baseline barking is manageable.
Yelling 'quiet!' when {dog_name} is in full bark mode — wait for a natural pause, then cue 'quiet' and treat.
troubleshooting: If 'quiet' isn't working, you may be rewarding too slow. The treat needs to come during the first second of silence, before the next bark starts.

title: Teach 'Back Up'
overview: Teach {dog_name} to walk backward on cue. This is useful for door manners, spatial awareness, and keeping {dog_name} out of your space when needed.
steps: Stand facing {dog_name} in a narrow hallway or between two pieces of furniture.
Walk slowly toward {dog_name}. Most dogs will naturally step backward.
The moment {dog_name} takes even one step back, mark 'yes!' and toss a treat behind them.
Repeat until {dog_name} consistently backs up when you walk forward.
Add the cue 'back up' or 'back' just before you step forward.
Gradually fade your forward movement until {dog_name} backs up on the verbal cue alone.
success_criteria: {dog_name} takes 3+ steps backward on verbal cue without you needing to walk toward them.
pro_tips: Toss the treat behind {dog_name} (in their backward direction) to reinforce the movement pattern. {breed_tip}
A hallway is the perfect training space for this — it prevents {dog_name} from turning around instead of backing up.
This is surprisingly useful for real life: kitchen doorway, getting off furniture, making space at the door.
common_mistakes: Lunging forward at {dog_name} — gentle, slow steps only. We want calm backward movement, not scared retreat.
Only practicing in the hallway. Once they know it, practice in open spaces too.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} sits instead of backing up, try holding a treat at their nose level and pushing slightly toward them (not down). This encourages backward stepping rather than sitting.

title: Teach 'Middle' (Between Your Legs)
overview: Teach {dog_name} to stand between your legs on cue. This is useful as a safety position in crowds, at the vet, or anytime you need {dog_name} contained and focused.
steps: Stand with your feet about shoulder-width apart. {dog_name} facing you.
Lure {dog_name} between your legs from front to back with a treat.
When they're standing between your legs facing forward, mark 'yes!' and treat between your legs.
Repeat 5 times with the lure. Then add the cue 'middle' before luring.
Gradually fade the lure until {dog_name} goes to middle position on verbal cue.
Practice holding 'middle' for 5 seconds, then 10, then 30. Treat for staying in position.
success_criteria: {dog_name} goes to middle position on cue and holds for 10 seconds.
pro_tips: This is invaluable at the vet's office — {dog_name} between your legs feels safe and protected. {breed_tip}
Start with wide leg placement and gradually bring legs closer as {dog_name} gets comfortable.
This works for small to medium dogs. Large dogs may learn a modified version (standing beside your leg).
common_mistakes: Luring too fast — {dog_name} should walk slowly through and stop between your legs.
Not rewarding the position itself — treat while {dog_name} is between your legs, not after they leave.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} is nervous about going between your legs, start by luring them under a chair first (similar concept, less intimidating).

title: Teach Sit with Luring
overview: Sit is the first and most useful command {dog_name} will learn. A reliable sit replaces jumping, rushing doors, and begging at the table.
steps: Hold a treat just above {dog_name}'s nose (close enough to smell, too high to snatch).
Slowly move the treat backward over their head toward their tail.
As their nose goes up, their butt goes down. The instant it touches the ground — 'yes!' + treat.
Repeat 10 times, then add the word 'sit' JUST BEFORE the lure motion.
Over 3-5 sessions, fade the lure: use an empty hand, then just the voice cue.
success_criteria: {dog_name} sits on verbal cue alone (no hand signal) 8 out of 10 times indoors.
pro_tips: If {dog_name} backs up instead of sitting, practice against a wall.
Short sessions (2-3 minutes) multiple times a day beat one long session.
common_mistakes: Pushing {dog_name}'s butt down — this is aversive and doesn't teach the behavior.
Saying 'sit, sit, sit' repeatedly — say it ONCE and wait.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} jumps for the treat instead of sitting, your hand is too high. Lower it closer to their nose and move more slowly.

title: Teach Sit with Capturing
overview: An alternative to luring — reward {dog_name} for sitting naturally throughout the day. This creates a dog who offers sits spontaneously because sitting = good things happen.
steps: Keep treats in your pocket throughout the day.
Every time {dog_name} sits on their own (not asked), immediately mark 'yes!' and treat.
Don't say anything before or during — just catch and reward the natural behavior.
After 2-3 days of catching sits, start saying 'sit' AS they're sitting.
After another 2-3 days, try saying 'sit' before they do it.
success_criteria: {dog_name} offers sits without being asked at least 5 times per day (check-ins, greetings, wanting food).
pro_tips: Capturing produces the most durable behaviors because {dog_name} thinks sitting was THEIR idea.
Use this method alongside luring for the fastest results.
common_mistakes: Missing natural sits because you weren't watching — keep treats accessible.
Only rewarding during formal training sessions.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} rarely sits on their own, they may be too excited. Wait for calm moments (after exercise, during quiet time) to catch the first few sits.

title: Teach Down (Lie Down)
overview: Down is harder than sit because it's a vulnerable position. Teach {dog_name} to lie down calmly — essential for settling at cafes, vet offices, and during visitors.
steps: Ask {dog_name} to sit first.
Hold a treat at their nose, then slowly lower it straight down to the ground between their front paws.
As they follow, their elbows should touch the ground. Mark 'yes!' and treat instantly.
If only their head goes down, wait — don't push. They'll figure it out.
Add the cue 'down' once the behavior is reliable with the lure.
success_criteria: {dog_name} lies down on verbal cue 7 out of 10 times indoors.
pro_tips: If {dog_name} stands up instead of lying down, try luring under a low table or chair — the ceiling forces the down.
Some {breed} dogs find down harder due to body structure. Be patient.
common_mistakes: Moving the treat too far forward — this makes them walk forward, not lie down.
Using 'lay down' instead of just 'down' — keep cues short and consistent.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} pops back up immediately, reward while they're still down. Toss a few treats on the ground between their paws to keep them there longer.

title: Teach Stay (Duration)
overview: Teach {dog_name} to hold a sit or down position for increasing amounts of time. This is the foundation of self-control.
steps: Ask {dog_name} to sit.
Wait 1 second. If they hold it, mark 'yes!' and treat.
Gradually increase the wait: 2 seconds, 3, 5, 8, 10, 15, 20, 30.
If {dog_name} breaks position, say 'oops,' reset them, and try a shorter duration.
Once reliable at 30 seconds, add the cue 'stay' before the pause.
success_criteria: {dog_name} holds a sit-stay for 30 seconds with you standing 3 feet away.
pro_tips: Vary the durations randomly (5, 2, 10, 3, 15) — don't always go longer. Easy reps build confidence.
Always return TO {dog_name} to reward. Don't call them out of the stay yet.
common_mistakes: Increasing time too fast — if {dog_name} fails twice in a row, make it easier.
Rewarding AFTER they get up — reward WHILE they're still in position.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} breaks every time you move, start by just shifting your weight. Build movement tolerance in tiny increments.

title: Teach Stay (Distance)
overview: Build on duration stay by adding physical distance between you and {dog_name}. This teaches them to hold position even when you walk away.
steps: Ask {dog_name} to sit-stay.
Take one small step backward. Return immediately and reward.
Gradually add steps: 2, 3, 5, then to the end of the room.
Mix distances randomly — don't always go farther.
If {dog_name} follows you, say 'oops,' bring them back, and try less distance.
success_criteria: {dog_name} holds stay while you walk 10 feet away and return.
pro_tips: Distance, duration, and distraction are the '3 Ds' of stay. Only increase ONE at a time.
Turn your back briefly as an intermediate challenge before adding distance.
common_mistakes: Increasing distance AND duration simultaneously — too hard.
Calling {dog_name} to you from a stay before they're ready — this poisons the stay cue.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} always breaks at a specific distance, reward just BEFORE that distance several times to build success at that threshold.

title: Teach Come (Recall) — Foundation
overview: A reliable recall can save {dog_name}'s life. Start indoors where success is almost guaranteed, then build to more challenging environments.
steps: With {dog_name} a few feet away (indoors), say their name + 'come!' in a happy voice.
As they move toward you, back up a few steps to increase their momentum.
When they reach you, mark 'yes!' and give 3-5 treats in a row (make it a party).
Repeat 5-10 times per session.
NEVER call {dog_name} to you for something they don't like (bath, crate, leaving the park).
success_criteria: {dog_name} comes immediately when called from 15 feet away indoors 9 out of 10 times.
pro_tips: Recall should ALWAYS be rewarded — even at 5 years old. It's that important.
Use their absolute favorite treat for recall only. Make 'come' the best word in {dog_name}'s vocabulary.
common_mistakes: Chasing {dog_name} when they don't come — this is a fun game (for them). Run AWAY instead.
Using 'come' when you know they won't respond — each ignored recall weakens the cue.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} starts coming but stops halfway, you're competing with something more interesting. Increase your treat value and decrease the distraction level.

title: Teach Leave It — Foundation
overview: Teach {dog_name} to back away from something they want. Essential for safety — dropped medication, food on the street, other animals.
steps: Hold a boring treat in your closed fist at {dog_name}'s nose level.
They'll paw, lick, and nose at it. Wait silently.
The instant they pull back or look away, mark 'yes!' and give a DIFFERENT, better treat from your other hand.
Repeat 10 times until they immediately look away from the fist.
Now add 'leave it' just before presenting the fist.
success_criteria: {dog_name} looks away from the closed fist within 2 seconds of hearing 'leave it.'
pro_tips: The rule: they NEVER get the thing you say 'leave it' about. The reward comes from somewhere else.
Practice with increasing temptation: closed fist → open palm → treat on the ground → treat on your shoe.
common_mistakes: Giving them the 'leave it' treat — this teaches 'wait for the thing you want.'
Snatching the item away instead of letting them choose to leave it.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} can't stop going for the fist, the treat inside is too high-value. Start with something boring (kibble) and reward with something amazing (chicken).

title: Teach Drop It
overview: Teach {dog_name} to release items from their mouth on cue. This prevents resource guarding and keeps everyone safe when they pick up something dangerous.
steps: Start a gentle tug game with {dog_name} using a rope toy.
While they're holding the toy, put a high-value treat right at their nose.
When they open their mouth to take the treat, the toy drops. Mark 'yes!' and give the treat.
Immediately offer the toy back as a bonus reward. Drop it doesn't mean the fun ends.
After 10 successful reps, add the cue 'drop it' just before showing the treat.
success_criteria: {dog_name} drops a held toy on verbal cue 8 out of 10 times.
pro_tips: Always trade UP — the treat should be better than what they're holding.
Returning the toy teaches {dog_name} that dropping things = getting something PLUS getting the original item back.
common_mistakes: Prying items from {dog_name}'s mouth — this builds resource guarding.
Never giving the item back — this teaches them to run away with things.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} won't drop for treats, the item they have is too high-value to start with. Practice with boring items first (ball, stick) before upgrading to their favorite things.

title: Teach Wait (Impulse Control)
overview: Different from stay — 'wait' means pause briefly before doing something. Used at doors, before meals, and getting out of the car.
steps: Hold {dog_name}'s food bowl at chest height.
Begin lowering it. If {dog_name} moves toward it, raise it back up.
When {dog_name} holds still (even for 1 second), lower the bowl to the ground.
Release with 'okay!' and let them eat.
Add the cue 'wait' once they understand: stillness = food comes down.
success_criteria: {dog_name} waits with food bowl on the ground for 5 seconds until released.
pro_tips: Use this before every meal — built-in daily practice.
Apply to doorways too: door opens only when {dog_name} is still.
common_mistakes: Making them wait too long early on — start with 1 second and build up.
Using 'stay' and 'wait' interchangeably — keep them as different cues for different situations.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} lunges the moment you lower the bowl, start by just tipping the bowl slightly. Reward even tiny impulse control wins.

title: Teach Look at Me (Focus)
overview: Eye contact is the foundation of attention. Teach {dog_name} to check in with you on cue — essential for walking past distractions, in vet offices, and before any other command.
steps: Hold a treat between your eyes.
When {dog_name} makes eye contact (even briefly), mark 'yes!' and treat.
Gradually increase the duration of eye contact: 1 second, 2, 3, 5.
Fade the treat lure: hold the treat at your side, reward eye contact with your empty hand near your face.
Add the cue 'look' or 'watch me' once reliable.
success_criteria: {dog_name} holds eye contact for 5 seconds on cue in a low-distraction environment.
pro_tips: Practice 'look at me' before meals, before walks, before play. It becomes a default check-in behavior.
In distracting environments, reward ANY glance your way — don't wait for sustained eye contact.
common_mistakes: Staring at {dog_name} waiting for them to look — use the lure to show them what you want first.
Only practicing in quiet rooms — this skill is most needed in high-distraction environments.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} won't look at your eyes, they may be stress-avoidant. Start by rewarding looks at your face (not necessarily direct eye contact). Build comfort gradually.

title: Teach Place (Go to Your Bed)
overview: Teach {dog_name} to go to a specific spot (bed, mat, crate) and settle there. This is your management tool for visitors, cooking, and calm time.
steps: Set {dog_name}'s bed 3 feet away.
Toss a treat onto the bed. When {dog_name} goes to get it, mark 'yes!'
Toss another treat on the bed while they're still on it.
After 5 reps, wait for {dog_name} to look at or move toward the bed on their own — then mark and treat.
Add the cue 'place' or 'bed' when they're reliably going to the bed.
success_criteria: {dog_name} goes to their bed on cue from 10 feet away and stays for 30 seconds.
pro_tips: Make the bed a magic treat zone — randomly drop treats on it throughout the day so {dog_name} checks it often.
Use a portable mat so you can bring 'place' to restaurants, friends' houses, and the vet.
common_mistakes: Only using 'place' as a timeout — it should be a positive, rewarding place to be.
Expecting duration too soon — build time gradually.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} won't stay on the bed, they may be too excited. Practice after exercise when they're naturally calmer. Or start with a raised cot — many dogs settle faster on elevated beds.

title: Reliable Recall with Long Line
overview: Take {dog_name}'s recall outdoors using a long line for safety. This bridges the gap between indoor recall and off-leash reliability.
steps: Attach a 20-30 foot long line to {dog_name}'s harness (NOT collar).
Let them sniff and explore. When they're not looking at you, call '{dog_name}, come!'
If they come, throw a party — multiple treats, praise, play.
If they don't respond, gently guide them in with the line (no jerking).
Practice 5-10 recalls per outdoor session, mixing in easy and harder moments.
success_criteria: {dog_name} comes on the first call from 20+ feet in a low-distraction outdoor environment 7 out of 10 times.
pro_tips: Let the long line drag — don't hold it taut. The line is a safety net, not a leash.
Call {dog_name} AWAY from mid-level distractions first (interesting smells, not other dogs).
common_mistakes: Yanking the long line — this punishes coming toward you.
Only calling when it's time to leave — recall should happen throughout the outing.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} ignores you outdoors, your treats aren't competing with the environment. Try real chicken, cheese, or hot dogs — whatever they go crazy for.

title: Teach Off (Get Off Furniture)
overview: Teach {dog_name} to get off furniture on cue without a battle. This gives you control over couch privileges while keeping the relationship positive.
steps: When {dog_name} is on the couch, show a treat and toss it on the floor.
As they jump down to get it, say "off" and mark "yes!"
Repeat 10 times until "off" predicts the toss.
Test: say "off" without showing the treat. When they jump down, treat from your pocket.
Use "off" consistently for all furniture — counters, beds, chairs.
success_criteria: {dog_name} gets off furniture on verbal cue 8 out of 10 times.
pro_tips: If you want {dog_name} on furniture sometimes, add an invitation cue like "up" so they learn to ask permission.
Never physically push them off — this creates conflict around furniture.
common_mistakes: Using "down" for both lie down AND get off furniture — these need separate cues.
Inconsistency — if sometimes they are allowed and sometimes not, they will never learn the rule.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} immediately jumps back up after getting off, redirect them to their bed or mat with a chew toy. Give them an alternative that is equally comfortable.

title: Emergency Recall (Whistle or Special Word)
overview: A backup recall for emergencies — a unique sound or word that {dog_name} has NEVER heard without an incredible reward. Save this for moments when safety is at stake.
steps: Choose a sound {dog_name} has never heard: a whistle, a specific word, a unique sound.
In a low-distraction environment, make the sound and immediately feed 10-15 small treats in a row. Party.
Repeat 3 times per session, 2 sessions per day for a week. Sound = jackpot. Every time. No exceptions.
Test in moderate distraction: make the sound. {dog_name} should come running.
ONLY use this cue in real emergencies. Overuse ruins it.
success_criteria: {dog_name} drops everything and sprints to you upon hearing the emergency recall sound.
pro_tips: This is a separate tool from your everyday recall. Think of it as a fire alarm — rarely used, always effective.
Refresh the association monthly: sound = jackpot, even if you do not need it.
common_mistakes: Using the emergency recall for everyday situations — this dilutes its power.
Not rewarding massively enough — this cue needs to predict the BEST reward {dog_name} has ever received.
troubleshooting: If the emergency recall stops working, you have used it too often or the reward was not extraordinary enough. Re-pair the sound with new, even higher-value rewards for a week.

title: Teach Name Recognition
overview: Before {dog_name} can learn any command, they need to reliably respond to their name. This is the foundation of all communication between you and your {breed}.
steps: Wait until {dog_name} is looking away from you in a quiet room.
Say '{dog_name}!' in a happy, clear voice. Just once — don't repeat.
The moment {dog_name} looks at you, mark 'yes!' and treat immediately.
Wait for {dog_name} to look away again, then repeat. Do 10 reps.
Practice in different rooms, then in the yard, gradually increasing distractions.
success_criteria: {dog_name} turns to look at you within 2 seconds of hearing their name 9 out of 10 times.
pro_tips: Never use {dog_name}'s name for scolding — the name must always predict something good.
{breed_tip}
If {dog_name} has a long name, use the short version consistently during training.
common_mistakes: Saying the name over and over ('Luna! Luna! Lunaaa!') — this teaches {dog_name} to ignore it.
Using the name when calling for something unpleasant like baths or nail trims.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} won't orient to their name, make a kissy noise first to get attention, then immediately say the name and treat. Fade the kissy noise over 3 sessions.

title: Teach Stay with Distractions
overview: Once {dog_name} can stay for duration and distance, add real-world distractions. This is what makes stay actually useful outside of the living room.
steps: Put {dog_name} in a stay in a quiet room. Drop a treat on the floor 3 feet away. If they hold, release and reward.
Bounce a ball once while {dog_name} is in stay. Reward for holding.
Have someone walk past. Then walk past while talking. Reward each hold.
Squeak a toy briefly. Reward the stay.
Take this outside: practice stay with people walking by, other dogs visible at a distance, and street noise.
Always return to {dog_name} to release — never call them out of stay during this exercise.
success_criteria: {dog_name} holds a 30-second stay with moderate distractions (person walking by, toy bouncing) 4 out of 5 times.
pro_tips: Only increase one variable at a time: duration OR distance OR distractions. Never all three.
{breed_tip}
If {dog_name} breaks, calmly reposition them and make the next rep easier.
common_mistakes: Going too fast — a dog who breaks repeatedly is being asked too much too soon.
Punishing a broken stay — instead, just reset and simplify.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} can't hold stay with any distraction, go back to basic stay and add the mildest distraction possible (a tissue dropping). Build from there.

title: Teach Down from Standing
overview: Most puppies learn down from sitting. Teach {dog_name} to drop into down from a standing position — this is more practical for real-world emergency situations.
steps: With {dog_name} standing, hold a treat at their nose level.
Slowly lower the treat straight down to the floor between their front paws.
If {dog_name} folds into down, mark 'yes!' and reward.
If {dog_name} only lowers their front end (play bow), hold the treat under your palm on the floor and wait. Most puppies will eventually slide into full down.
Practice until {dog_name} drops from standing 8 out of 10 times with the lure.
Add the verbal cue 'down' just before you lure.
success_criteria: {dog_name} drops into down from a standing position on verbal cue 8 out of 10 times.
pro_tips: Practice on a non-slip surface — slippery floors make down uncomfortable for {dog_name}.
{breed_tip}
This is a precursor to emergency drop on walks.
common_mistakes: Pushing {dog_name} down physically — this creates resistance and distrust.
Moving the treat too fast — {dog_name}'s nose should track it inch by inch.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} backs up instead of lying down, practice against a wall so they can't retreat. The wall blocks backward movement and encourages the fold-back into down.

title: Teach Come When Called — Outdoors
overview: Recall outdoors is entirely different from indoors. The distractions are real, the stakes are higher, and {dog_name} needs to learn that coming to you is always the best option.
steps: Attach a 15-30 foot long line to {dog_name}'s harness. Go to a fenced or safe area.
Let {dog_name} explore and sniff. Wait until they're not looking at you.
Call '{dog_name}, come!' in a happy, excited voice. Run backwards a few steps.
When {dog_name} reaches you, throw a party — multiple treats, praise, play. Make it the best thing ever.
Let them go explore again. Repeat 5-8 times per session.
Never pull them in with the line unless it's a safety emergency. The line is a safety net, not a retrieval tool.
success_criteria: {dog_name} responds to outdoor recall 7 out of 10 times without line tension, with moderate distractions.
pro_tips: Use your highest-value treats for outdoor recall — this is the most important command your dog will ever learn.
{breed_tip}
Never call {dog_name} and then do something they don't like (leashing up to leave). Recall must always predict good things.
common_mistakes: Repeating 'come come come' — say it once. If they don't respond, go get them with the line.
Calling {dog_name} to punish them or end the fun. This poisons your recall.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} ignores you outdoors, the treats aren't high enough value. Try real chicken, hot dogs, or cheese. If still ignoring, reduce the distraction level and build back up.

title: Teach Leave It — Advanced (Moving Objects)
overview: Upgrade {dog_name}'s leave it from stationary items to things that move — rolling treats, dropped food, squirrels at a distance. This is real-world leave it.
steps: Roll a low-value treat slowly across the floor. Say 'leave it' before it reaches {dog_name}.
When {dog_name} stops or looks at you, mark 'yes!' and reward with a higher-value treat from your hand.
Increase difficulty: bounce a ball, toss a treat past them, have someone drop food.
On leash outdoors: practice 'leave it' with food wrappers, other dogs' toys, sticks.
Ultimate test: drop a piece of food while walking. Say 'leave it' before {dog_name} reaches it.
success_criteria: {dog_name} redirects attention from a moving/falling item to you on 'leave it' 7 out of 10 times.
pro_tips: The reward for leaving it must always be BETTER than the thing they're leaving. {breed_tip}
Practice 'leave it' with increasingly tempting items as {dog_name} improves.
This could save {dog_name}'s life — chicken bones, chocolate, medications on the sidewalk.
common_mistakes: Making 'leave it' the same value as what they're leaving — there's no incentive to comply.
Practicing only in the house. Real leave it needs outdoor repetitions.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} snatches items before you can cue 'leave it,' use a leash for control and increase the distance between {dog_name} and the item.

title: Teach Go to Your Crate on Cue
overview: Turn the crate into a place {dog_name} goes voluntarily on command. This links crate training with obedience and makes crating feel like a rewarded behavior, not a punishment.
steps: Stand a few feet from the open crate with {dog_name} beside you.
Toss a treat into the crate and say 'crate' or 'kennel up' as {dog_name} walks in to get it.
The moment they're inside, give 2-3 bonus treats through the crate door.
Release with 'free!' — no treat on the way out. All the good stuff happens INSIDE.
Practice until {dog_name} runs to the crate on the verbal cue alone, before you toss the treat.
Gradually add the door closing for 5 seconds, then 30 seconds, then minutes.
success_criteria: {dog_name} goes to crate on verbal cue from 10 feet away without a tossed lure.
pro_tips: Practice this before meals — use part of {dog_name}'s dinner as the crate reward.
{breed_tip}
This is useful for visitors, deliveries, mealtimes, and emergencies.
common_mistakes: Only cueing the crate when you're leaving — {dog_name} learns crate = alone. Practice when you're home too.
Letting {dog_name} out when they whine — wait for even 2 seconds of quiet, then release.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} won't enter voluntarily, go back to crate games (ex-crate-004). The crate needs more positive association before you can cue entry.

title: Teach Sit from Down Position
overview: Most puppies learn sit from standing. Teaching {dog_name} to pop into sit from lying down rounds out the skill and makes sit reliable from any position.
steps: With {dog_name} in a down position, hold a treat at their nose.
Slowly lift the treat up and slightly back toward their ears.
{dog_name} should rock back into a sit as they follow the treat upward.
Mark 'yes!' the moment their butt hits the floor and treat.
Practice until they pop from down to sit fluidly on a verbal 'sit' cue.
Chain it: sit → down → sit → down. Treat each transition.
success_criteria: {dog_name} moves from down to sit on verbal cue 8 out of 10 times.
pro_tips: If {dog_name} stands up instead of sitting, you're lifting the treat too far forward. Keep it close to their nose.
{breed_tip}
Chaining sit-down-sit is great for building focus and earning quick XP.
common_mistakes: Moving the treat too fast — {dog_name} overshoots into a stand.
Always starting from standing — {dog_name} needs to learn sit means sit from ANY position.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} won't rock back from down, try practicing with their back against a wall so standing isn't an option.

title: Teach 'Enough' or 'All Done'
overview: Give {dog_name} a clear signal that an activity is over. This prevents demand barking, nudging for more play, and confusion about when training or play time has ended.
steps: At the end of a play or training session, hold both hands up (palms out) and say 'all done!' or 'enough!' clearly.
Turn your body slightly away. Ignore any attempts to re-engage for 10 seconds.
If {dog_name} sits or settles, mark 'yes!' and give a final treat.
Walk away calmly. The activity is over.
Be consistent — always use the same word/gesture to end interactions.
Practice ending play sessions, training, petting, and treat time with this cue.
success_criteria: {dog_name} stops trying to re-engage within 10 seconds of the 'all done' cue.
pro_tips: This cue prevents 'just one more' syndrome that leads to overstimulated puppies.
{breed_tip}
Children in the household should learn this cue too — it helps everyone communicate clearly.
common_mistakes: Saying 'all done' and then continuing to engage — {dog_name} learns it means nothing.
Using 'no' instead of 'all done' — 'no' is punitive; 'all done' is informational.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} barks or nudges after 'all done,' turn your back completely and wait. Any attention (even 'stop it') reinforces the demand behavior.

title: Teach Sit-Stay for Real Life (Duration Build)
overview: Extend {dog_name}'s sit-stay to 2+ minutes in everyday situations: while you tie your shoes, answer the door, or prepare their food.
steps: Cue 'sit' then 'stay.' Stand right in front of {dog_name}.
Wait 10 seconds. Return, treat, release with 'free.'
Repeat, adding 5-10 seconds each rep. Go to 30 seconds, then 1 minute.
Start doing normal activities during the stay: take a step to the side, bend down to touch your shoe, pick something up.
Build to 2 minutes with you performing a mundane task (folding a towel, checking your phone).
If {dog_name} breaks, silently reposition and start the timer over at a shorter duration.
success_criteria: {dog_name} holds a sit-stay for 2 minutes while you perform a simple activity 4 out of 5 times.
pro_tips: Real-life stay is more useful than competition stay. Focus on practical scenarios.
{breed_tip}
Reward at random intervals during the stay to keep {dog_name} guessing — don't always wait until the end.
common_mistakes: Always releasing from the same distance — vary where you stand when you release.
Making every stay longer than the last — throw in easy short stays to keep confidence high.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} consistently breaks at the same duration (e.g., 45 seconds), do multiple reps at 30 seconds to build confidence before pushing past the breaking point.

title: Teach 'Go Say Hi' (Polite Greeting Permission)
overview: Give {dog_name} a release cue that means 'you may now greet that person or dog.' This builds impulse control by making greetings something they earn, not something they lunge toward.
steps: When approaching a person {dog_name} wants to greet, cue 'sit' about 6 feet away.
Wait for {dog_name} to sit and look at you. Mark 'yes!'
Say 'go say hi!' in a happy voice and walk forward to let {dog_name} approach the person.
If {dog_name} jumps, immediately back up and restart. The greeting ends if manners break.
After a brief greeting (10-15 seconds), call {dog_name} back to you with 'come' and treat.
Practice with family first, then friends, then strangers who are willing to help.
success_criteria: {dog_name} sits and waits for the 'go say hi' cue before approaching people 7 out of 10 times.
pro_tips: The greeting itself is the reward — you don't always need treats for this one.
{breed_tip}
Tell the helper person to ignore {dog_name} if they jump. Attention only comes for four-on-the-floor.
common_mistakes: Letting {dog_name} greet while lunging — this teaches that pulling = access to people.
Forcing greetings with people or dogs who make {dog_name} uncomfortable.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} can't hold a sit near people, you're too close. Start at 15-20 feet and gradually decrease distance over multiple sessions.

title: Teach 'Touch' (Nose Target)
overview: Teach {dog_name} to touch their nose to your palm on cue. This versatile skill becomes a recall alternative, a redirect tool, a confidence builder, and the foundation for dozens of tricks.
steps: Hold your flat palm out about 6 inches from {dog_name}'s nose.
Most puppies will naturally investigate and bump your hand with their nose. The instant they do, mark 'yes!' and treat.
If {dog_name} doesn't touch, rub a tiny bit of treat scent on your palm to encourage it.
Repeat 10 times. Then add the cue 'touch' just before presenting your palm.
Gradually move your hand to different positions — high, low, to the side — so {dog_name} has to move to touch.
Build distance: start touching from 1 foot away, then 3 feet, then across the room.
success_criteria: {dog_name} touches their nose to your palm within 2 seconds from 5 feet away, 8 out of 10 times.
pro_tips: Touch is the secret weapon for nervous dogs — touching your hand gives them a job and builds confidence.
{breed_tip}
Use touch to move {dog_name} without physically pushing or pulling them.
common_mistakes: Reaching your hand toward {dog_name}'s face instead of holding still and letting them come to you.
Only practicing at nose height — vary the position so {dog_name} learns to seek your hand.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} tries to lick or mouth your palm instead of nose-bumping, pull your hand away for any lick and only mark a dry nose boop.

title: The Yelp and Redirect Method
overview: Teach {dog_name} that human skin is sensitive by mimicking how littermates communicate pain. This is the foundation of bite inhibition.
steps: Play with {dog_name} normally using your hands.
When teeth touch skin (even gently), make a sharp 'OW!' sound and freeze all movement.
Wait 3 seconds. {dog_name} should pull back or look confused.
Immediately redirect to an appropriate chew toy.
When {dog_name} chews the toy instead, praise and continue playing.
success_criteria: {dog_name} reduces hard biting by 50% within 5 days. Soft mouthing is normal and expected.
pro_tips: Start by only reacting to HARD bites. Once those stop, react to medium bites. Then gentle mouthing. Gradual is better.
Keep a chew toy in your pocket at all times during the teething phase.
common_mistakes: Pulling your hand away fast — this triggers chase instinct and makes biting more fun.
Being inconsistent — every family member must respond the same way.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} gets more excited when you yelp, skip the sound and just freeze + redirect silently. Some puppies interpret yelping as play.

title: Timeout Protocol for Persistent Biting
overview: When redirection isn't enough, a brief timeout teaches {dog_name} that biting ends all fun. This is not punishment — it's removing the reward.
steps: If {dog_name} bites hard after redirection, calmly say 'too bad' and stand up.
Leave the room (or step behind a baby gate) for 15-30 seconds.
Return calmly and resume play.
If {dog_name} bites hard again within 1 minute, repeat the timeout — this time 30-60 seconds.
After 3 timeouts in one session, end the play session entirely.
success_criteria: Timeouts needed per play session drop from 3+ to 0-1 within two weeks.
pro_tips: Leaving the room is more powerful than removing {dog_name} from the room. YOU are the reward.
Baby gates let you exit without fumbling with doors.
common_mistakes: Making the timeout too long — 15-30 seconds is enough. Puppies have short memories.
Getting angry or physical — stay calm and matter-of-fact.
troubleshooting: If biting is worst in the evening, {dog_name} is likely overtired. Enforce naps (1 hour awake, 2 hours napping is typical for young puppies).

title: Chew Toy Rotation System
overview: Keep {dog_name}'s mouth busy with appropriate chew toys. A good rotation prevents boredom and redirects the natural urge to chew.
steps: Get 6-8 different chew toys: rubber, rope, fabric, frozen, interactive.
Put out 3 at a time. Rotate every 2-3 days.
When {dog_name} chews an appropriate toy, praise quietly: 'good chewing!'
Keep a frozen Kong or wet cloth in the freezer for teething relief.
Always have one chew within arm's reach during high-energy periods.
success_criteria: {dog_name} independently chooses a chew toy instead of furniture or hands 4 out of 5 times.
pro_tips: Frozen items are gold during teething — freeze a wet washcloth, a Kong with peanut butter, or rubber toys.
Match toy hardness to age: soft rubber for young puppies, harder as they grow.
common_mistakes: Giving all toys at once — novelty is what keeps toys interesting.
Choosing toys that look like household items (old shoes, socks).
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} ignores toys for furniture, make toys more interesting: stuff with treats, add peanut butter, or play with the toy yourself first to create excitement.

title: Hand Targeting (Touch)
overview: Teach {dog_name} to touch your hand with their nose instead of biting it. This gives them a job for their mouth and is a foundation skill for many future exercises.
steps: Hold your flat palm 6 inches from {dog_name}'s nose.
Most puppies will naturally investigate and touch your hand with their nose.
The instant their nose touches your palm, mark 'yes!' and treat.
Repeat 10 times per session, gradually moving your hand to different positions.
Once reliable, add the cue 'touch' just before presenting your hand.
success_criteria: {dog_name} touches your palm on cue 8 out of 10 times in a low-distraction environment.
pro_tips: This trick turns 'hand = bite target' into 'hand = nose target.' It's a behavioral redirect built into muscle memory.
Use this as an alternative to biting during greetings — ask for 'touch' before they default to nipping.
common_mistakes: Holding the treat in the target hand — the hand itself should be the target.
Moving your hand toward {dog_name} — let THEM come to you.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} licks or bites your hand instead of nose-touching, close your hand and reset. Only mark a clean nose touch.

title: Gentle Mouth Training
overview: Teach {dog_name} to take treats gently from your hand. This builds awareness of mouth pressure and translates to softer mouthing overall.
steps: Hold a treat in your closed fist at {dog_name}'s nose level.
If they paw, lick, or nudge gently, open your hand and let them take it.
If they bite or grab roughly, close your fist and say 'gentle.'
Wait 3 seconds, then try again.
Gradually, only open for the gentlest takes.
success_criteria: {dog_name} takes treats from an open hand without teeth touching skin 8 out of 10 times.
pro_tips: Flat palm = easier for {dog_name} to be gentle. Fingers = harder. Start with palm.
Practice with all family members, especially children.
common_mistakes: Snatching your hand away — this triggers grab-faster instinct.
Using hard treats that require more grabbing force.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} is always too grabby, hold the treat between thumb and forefinger and only release when they use a soft mouth. This makes gentle = rewarded and rough = nothing happens.

title: Puppy Play Rules for Children
overview: Teach kids and {dog_name} safe play rules. Children are the most common targets of puppy mouthing because they move fast and squeal — both triggers for bite behavior.
steps: Teach children: no running past {dog_name}, no high-pitched screaming, no pulling ears or tail.
Show kids how to play with toys — drag a rope toy for {dog_name} to chase instead of hands or feet.
Practice the freeze rule: if {dog_name} mouths, the child should become a tree — arms crossed, look away, stand still.
Supervise ALL interactions between children and {dog_name}. No exceptions.
Give {dog_name} breaks from children every 15-20 minutes — puppies get overstimulated.
success_criteria: {dog_name} plays with children using toys, not teeth, for 10+ minute supervised sessions.
pro_tips: Children under 6 should never be alone with any puppy, regardless of breed or temperament.
Give {dog_name} a safe space (crate or room) that is off-limits to children — they need escape options.
common_mistakes: Expecting young children to follow the rules consistently — adult supervision is always needed.
Punishing {dog_name} for mouthing during excited play — redirect and manage instead.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} targets children specifically, they may be overstimulated by kid energy. Increase crate nap time and limit child-dog interaction to calm, structured activities only.

title: Structured Tug Play
overview: Tug is one of the best games for teaching {dog_name} mouth rules — when to bite and when to release. It builds impulse control while letting {breed} puppies use their mouths appropriately.
steps: Present the tug toy and say 'take it' in an excited voice. Wiggle it on the ground like prey.
Let {dog_name} grab it and play tug for 5-10 seconds. Keep it low — no lifting {dog_name} off the ground.
Freeze completely. Hold the toy still and say 'drop it' or 'out.' Wait.
The moment {dog_name} releases, mark 'yes!' and immediately restart the game — the game IS the reward.
If teeth touch your hand at any point, yelp and the game stops for 30 seconds.
Play 5-6 rounds, always ending on a successful 'drop it' with a bonus treat.
success_criteria: {dog_name} releases the tug toy within 3 seconds of 'drop it' and never redirects teeth to hands.
pro_tips: Let {dog_name} win sometimes — it builds confidence and keeps the game fun.
{breed_tip}
Tug is NOT about dominance. Modern science confirms it has zero negative behavioral effects.
common_mistakes: Yanking the toy away instead of freezing and waiting — this teaches {dog_name} to grip harder.
Playing with socks or clothing items — {dog_name} can't distinguish 'toy sock' from 'your sock.'
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} won't release, hold a treat to their nose while holding the toy still. As they drop the toy to take the treat, say 'drop it.' Gradually fade the treat lure.

title: Impulse Control Around Hands
overview: Teach {dog_name} that hands are never appropriate chew targets. Hands approaching means good things — treats, pets, play — but only when {dog_name}'s mouth stays soft.
steps: Hold a treat in your closed fist. Present your fist to {dog_name}.
If {dog_name} bites, mouths, or paws aggressively — keep your fist closed. Say nothing. Wait.
The moment {dog_name} backs off, sniffs gently, or licks — mark 'yes!' and open your hand to deliver the treat.
Gradually make it harder: hold the treat in an open palm. Close it if {dog_name} lunges. Open when they wait.
Practice with both hands, different people, and while moving your hands around.
success_criteria: {dog_name} waits calmly with a still mouth when a treat is presented in an open palm.
pro_tips: Wear a garden glove if {dog_name}'s teeth are sharp and you're flinching — your flinch can confuse the lesson.
{breed_tip}
This exercise directly translates to polite treat-taking from strangers.
common_mistakes: Pulling your hand away when {dog_name} bites — this triggers chase instinct and makes it worse.
Rewarding too slow — the treat must come the instant {dog_name} shows restraint.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} is too excited to think, lower the value of the treat (use kibble) or practice after a play session when they're slightly tired.

title: Bite Pressure Calibration
overview: Before eliminating biting entirely, teach {dog_name} to control pressure. This is critical safety training — a dog who learned soft mouth as a puppy is far less dangerous as an adult.
steps: During calm play, let {dog_name} mouth your hand gently. Allow soft contact.
The moment you feel moderate pressure (it should not hurt), yelp 'ow!' and freeze for 3 seconds.
If {dog_name} softens, resume play. If they bite harder, stand up and turn away for 15 seconds.
Over 2 weeks, gradually lower your tolerance — yelp at lighter and lighter pressure.
By week 3, any tooth contact on skin = yelp and brief timeout.
By week 4, only soft mouthing OR no mouthing should remain. Most puppies choose to stop entirely.
success_criteria: {dog_name} never applies more than gentle pressure when mouthing, and frequency decreases week over week.
pro_tips: This is a gradual process. Don't jump to zero tolerance immediately — {dog_name} needs to learn calibration first.
{breed_tip}
Involve all family members — everyone must have the same threshold or {dog_name} gets confused.
common_mistakes: Allowing hard bites during 'play' but punishing them during calm time — be consistent regardless of context.
Skipping this step and going straight to 'no mouthing ever' — pressure calibration is more important than elimination.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} is a very mouthy {breed}, expect this to take 4-6 weeks. That's normal. Focus on pressure reduction, not elimination. Elimination comes after calibration.

title: Managing Teething Pain (4-6 Months)
overview: When adult teeth come in, {dog_name} will chew more intensely. This isn't regression — it's pain management. Provide appropriate outlets and protect your furniture.
steps: Soak a washcloth in water or low-sodium broth, twist it, and freeze it. Offer to {dog_name} to gnaw on.
Prepare frozen Kongs with peanut butter or yogurt for longer chewing sessions.
Rotate 3-4 different texture chew toys daily so {dog_name} doesn't get bored of any single one.
When you catch {dog_name} chewing something inappropriate, calmly redirect to an approved chew.
If mouthing on people increases during teething, increase frozen chew availability — {dog_name} is in pain, not regressing.
Check {dog_name}'s gums weekly for loose baby teeth. Mild bleeding is normal.
success_criteria: {dog_name} defaults to chewing approved items 80%+ of the time and mouthing on people does not increase during teething.
pro_tips: Frozen carrots are a cheap, healthy teething toy most puppies love.
{breed_tip}
Teething typically peaks at 4-5 months and resolves by 7 months when all 42 adult teeth are in.
common_mistakes: Assuming increased chewing is a training failure — it's a physiological phase.
Giving old shoes or socks as chew toys — {dog_name} can't distinguish old from new.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} refuses frozen items, try slightly chilled instead of fully frozen. Some puppies don't like extreme cold. Also try different textures — rope, rubber, nylabone.

title: Crate Introduction
overview: Make the crate {dog_name}'s happy place — not a jail. A properly introduced crate becomes their safe den for sleeping, calming down, and travel.
steps: Place the crate in a common area with the door open and a comfy blanket inside.
Toss treats near the crate entrance. Let {dog_name} choose to approach.
Toss treats just inside the door. Then toward the back.
When {dog_name} goes fully inside, mark 'yes!' and toss treats inside.
Do NOT close the door yet. Repeat this game 3-4 times per day for 2-3 days.
success_criteria: {dog_name} voluntarily enters the crate and lingers inside for 10+ seconds.
pro_tips: Feed meals in the crate (door open). This is the single strongest crate-positive association.
Never use the crate as punishment. Ever.
common_mistakes: Forcing {dog_name} into the crate — this creates crate aversion that takes weeks to fix.
Closing the door on the first day — rushing builds anxiety.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} won't go near the crate, it may be in a scary location (too isolated, next to loud appliances). Move it to where the family hangs out. You can also try removing the door temporarily.

title: Building Crate Duration
overview: Gradually increase the time {dog_name} spends in the crate with the door closed. Slow and steady prevents crate anxiety.
steps: Give {dog_name} a stuffed Kong, close the crate door.
Stay visible. After 30 seconds, open the door calmly (no fanfare).
Repeat, adding 30 seconds each time: 1 min, 2 min, 5 min, 10 min.
If {dog_name} whines, wait for a 3-second pause in whining before opening.
After a week, start briefly leaving the room while they're in the crate.
success_criteria: {dog_name} stays calm in the crate for 30 minutes with you in another room.
pro_tips: A frozen Kong keeps them busy 20-30 minutes. Time the door-closed period to finish when the Kong does.
Cover the crate with a blanket (leaving one side open) for a den-like feel.
common_mistakes: Opening the door when they cry — this rewards crying. Wait for silence.
Crating for too long — maximum hours = age in months + 1 (3-month puppy = 4 hours max).
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} panics in the crate, you went too fast. Go back to door-open feeding and rebuild. Some puppies need 2 weeks of door-open before any door-closed practice.

title: Nighttime Crate Training
overview: Help {dog_name} sleep through the night in their crate. The first few nights are the hardest, but consistency pays off within a week.
steps: Place the crate next to your bed (so {dog_name} can see/smell/hear you).
Exhaust {dog_name} with play 30 minutes before bedtime.
Final potty trip. Then into the crate with a small chew.
If they whine, place your fingers against the crate door so they know you're there.
Set a pre-emptive alarm for nighttime potty (see nighttime potty exercise).
success_criteria: {dog_name} settles in the crate within 10 minutes of bedtime and sleeps 4+ hours.
pro_tips: A ticking clock or heartbeat toy mimics littermates and soothes new puppies.
Gradually move the crate farther from your bed over 2-3 weeks to its final location.
common_mistakes: Putting the crate in another room on night one — too much change at once.
Letting {dog_name} out when they cry at 3am to play — nighttime is boring and business-only.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} cries inconsolably for 30+ minutes every night for a week, they may have separation anxiety. Try an exercise pen (more space) instead of a crate, and consult your vet.

title: Crate Games for Positive Association
overview: Turn the crate into a game zone. These quick games make {dog_name} LOVE going into their crate — running in voluntarily instead of being placed in.
steps: Toss a treat into the crate. When {dog_name} runs in, say "crate!" and toss 3 more treats inside.
Let them come out freely. Wait 5 seconds. Toss another treat in. Repeat.
Start hiding a favorite toy in the crate. Let {dog_name} discover it.
Feed one meal per day entirely inside the crate with the door open.
Play a "crate dash" game: say "crate!" and race {dog_name} to the crate. Whoever gets there first wins (let them win).
success_criteria: {dog_name} runs into the crate eagerly on cue, with tail wagging.
pro_tips: The crate should be the most rewarding spot in the house. Random treat deliveries build this.
Leave the crate door open during the day so {dog_name} can choose to nap there.
common_mistakes: Only using the crate when leaving the house — this predicts isolation.
Closing the door every time they enter during games — keep it casual.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} avoids the crate, place it in a different location and start the introduction process from scratch. Some puppies need a fresh start in a new spot.

title: Crate Training with Meals
overview: Feed {dog_name}'s meals inside the crate to build the strongest positive association possible. Food is the most powerful motivator — link it to the crate every single day.
steps: Place {dog_name}'s food bowl just inside the crate door for the first 2 meals.
Next 2 meals: move the bowl to the middle of the crate.
Once comfortable, move the bowl all the way to the back of the crate.
After {dog_name} walks all the way in comfortably, gently close the door while they eat.
Open the door the moment they finish eating. Gradually extend the closed-door time by 1-2 minutes per meal.
Within a week, {dog_name} should eat all meals in the crate with the door closed for 5-10 minutes after finishing.
success_criteria: {dog_name} enters the crate eagerly at mealtime and stays calm with the door closed until released.
pro_tips: Use a Kong stuffed with food to extend the time {dog_name} is happily occupied in the crate.
{breed_tip}
If {dog_name} whines after finishing, wait for 3 seconds of quiet before opening. Never open during a whine.
common_mistakes: Only putting {dog_name} in the crate when you leave — meals-in-crate prevents this negative association.
Skipping this step because {dog_name} eats fine outside — meal time is your best crate training tool.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} won't eat inside the crate, scatter kibble in a trail leading into the crate. Start with the bowl outside, just next to the crate, and move it closer daily.

title: Separation Building in the Crate
overview: Teach {dog_name} to be calm in the crate while you're in another room. This is the bridge between 'crate while you're visible' and 'crate while you're gone.'
steps: Give {dog_name} a stuffed Kong in the crate with the door closed.
Stay in view for the first 5 minutes. Treat through the door for calm behavior.
Step out of sight for 5 seconds. Return before {dog_name} reacts. Treat through the door.
Gradually increase out-of-sight time: 10 seconds, 30 seconds, 1 minute, 3 minutes, 5 minutes.
Don't make a big deal of departures or returns. Casual is key.
If {dog_name} whines while you're out of sight, wait for a pause in whining, then return. Never return during active whining.
success_criteria: {dog_name} stays quiet in the crate for 15+ minutes while you're in another room.
pro_tips: Leave a recently-worn t-shirt near the crate — your scent is calming.
{breed_tip}
White noise or calming music can help mask departure sounds that trigger anxiety.
common_mistakes: Going from 'in the room' to 'gone for 2 hours' — this is too big a jump. Baby steps.
Making arrivals exciting — a calm return teaches {dog_name} that comings and goings are no big deal.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} panics the moment you leave the room, you need more foundation work. Practice stepping behind a chair for 1 second, then returning. Build from there.

title: Crate Training for Car Travel
overview: Get {dog_name} comfortable being crated in the car. A crated dog in a vehicle is safer for everyone and prevents car anxiety from developing.
steps: Place the crate in the car with the engine off. Let {dog_name} explore with treats.
Feed a meal or Kong in the car crate with the engine off. Door closed for the meal duration.
Next session: engine running, parked. Kong in crate. 5-10 minutes.
Short drive (2-3 minutes) with {dog_name} in the crate. Return home, treat party.
Gradually increase drive length: 5 minutes, 10 minutes, 20 minutes.
Destination should be somewhere fun initially — not the vet.
success_criteria: {dog_name} rides calmly in the car crate for a 20-minute drive without whining or distress.
pro_tips: Secure the crate with a seatbelt or cargo straps — a loose crate is dangerous.
{breed_tip}
Some puppies do better with a crate cover in the car to reduce visual stimulation.
common_mistakes: First car trip being a long drive to the vet — this creates lasting car anxiety.
Letting {dog_name} ride loose in the car 'because they're small' — unsafe at any size.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} drools, vomits, or shakes in the car, they may have motion sickness. Ask your vet about anti-nausea options and keep drives very short until they grow out of it.

title: Handling Crate Crying (Extinction Protocol)
overview: Most puppies cry in the crate initially. This exercise teaches you how to respond without reinforcing the behavior or traumatizing {dog_name}.
steps: Ensure {dog_name} has been pottied, exercised, and has a Kong or chew before crating.
Close the crate door and move to another room where you can hear but not see {dog_name}.
When crying starts, DO NOTHING. No talking, no shushing, no returning to the room.
Wait for any pause in crying — even 3 seconds of silence.
During that pause, calmly return and deliver a treat through the crate. Don't open the door.
Leave again. Wait for silence. Treat silence. Repeat.
Over days, extend the required silence before treating: 10 seconds, 30 seconds, 1 minute.
success_criteria: {dog_name} settles within 5 minutes of being crated with minimal or no crying.
pro_tips: The first night is the hardest. Most puppies improve dramatically by night 3. {breed_tip}
Set your phone timer to track actual cry duration — it always feels longer than it is.
If crying lasts longer than 15 continuous minutes, take {dog_name} out for a potty break (no play), then re-crate.
common_mistakes: Opening the crate during crying — this teaches {dog_name} that crying = freedom.
Yelling 'quiet!' at the crate — any attention is reinforcement to a lonely puppy.
troubleshooting: If crying hasn't improved after 5 days, the crate may not have enough positive association yet. Go back to crate games and meal feeding. Also ensure the crate isn't too big — puppies feel more secure in snug spaces.

title: Crate Duration Building (1-4 Hours)
overview: Gradually build {dog_name}'s ability to stay in the crate for longer periods during the day. A puppy who can do 3-4 hours is ready for real-world alone time.
steps: Start with 30 minutes while you're home. Give a Kong and go about your business.
If {dog_name} is calm at 30 minutes, try 1 hour the next day.
At 1 hour, include a brief potty break, then re-crate for another 30 minutes.
Build to 2 hours, then 3, then 4 — always including potty breaks appropriate for age.
Maximum crate time rule: puppy's age in months + 1 = max hours. (3 months = 4 hours max.)
Never exceed the max, even if {dog_name} seems fine — bladder capacity is the limiting factor.
success_criteria: {dog_name} stays calm in the crate for up to 4 hours (age-appropriate) with a potty break.
pro_tips: Always potty immediately before and after crating. Build the association: out of crate = potty first.
{breed_tip}
Rotate crate toys so there's always something 'new' to investigate.
common_mistakes: Crating for 8 hours because {dog_name} 'doesn't complain' — just because they're quiet doesn't mean their bladder is okay.
Only crating when you leave — practice while you're home so it's not a departure cue.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} has accidents in the crate, the crate may be too big (they can potty in one corner and sleep in another). Use a divider to make the space snug.

title: Transitioning from Crate to Free Roam
overview: When {dog_name} has proven trustworthy, begin expanding their free space. This is a graduation moment — but it must be done gradually.
steps: Choose one puppy-proofed room. Remove anything chewable, toxic, or valuable.
Leave {dog_name} in this room (not the crate) for 15 minutes while you're home. Observe via camera or check frequently.
If no destruction after 15 minutes, try 30 minutes. Then 1 hour.
After 3-4 successful days at 1 hour, try leaving the house for 30 minutes with {dog_name} in the room.
Gradually expand to longer durations and eventually additional rooms.
If any regression (chewing, accidents), go back to crate for 1-2 weeks and try again.
success_criteria: {dog_name} stays calm and non-destructive in a single room for 2+ hours without supervision.
pro_tips: Use a pet camera to monitor — many puppies are fine but it's good to verify. {breed_tip}
Leave safe chew toys available to redirect any chewing urge.
Most puppies aren't ready for free roam until 6-12 months minimum.
common_mistakes: Giving full house access too soon — one unsupervised puppy + one pair of shoes = learned bad habit.
Skipping the camera step — you won't know about stress behaviors you can't see.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} destroys things when left alone, this may be separation anxiety rather than just boredom. Talk to your vet if destruction happens only when you leave.

title: Paw Handling and Nail Prep
overview: Before you ever clip a nail, teach {dog_name} that paw handling is no big deal. This prevents the nail-trimming drama that plagues most dog owners.
steps: Touch {dog_name}'s shoulder. Treat. Touch elbow. Treat. Touch paw. Treat.
Hold paw for 1 second. Treat. 2 seconds. Treat. 5 seconds. Treat.
Gently spread toes. Treat. Touch individual nails. Treat.
Show the clippers. Treat. Touch clippers to paw. Treat.
Clip ONE nail (just the very tip). Throw a treat party. Done for today.
success_criteria: {dog_name} allows all four paws to be held for 10 seconds and nails touched without pulling away.
pro_tips: Do only 1-2 nails per session. There's no rule that says you must do all nails at once.
Use a nail file or Dremel as an alternative — some dogs prefer the sensation.
common_mistakes: Restraining {dog_name} forcefully — this creates a lifelong nail trim battle.
Trying to do all nails in one session — patience now saves years of stress.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} is already traumatized by nail trims, start over with just paw touches. Don't even bring out the clippers for 2 weeks. Rebuild the positive association from scratch.

title: Tooth Brushing Introduction
overview: Start dental care early. Puppies who learn to enjoy tooth brushing have far fewer dental problems as adults — and dental disease is the #1 health issue in dogs.
steps: Let {dog_name} lick dog-safe toothpaste off your finger. (It's flavored — they'll love it.)
Lift their lip and rub your finger along the outside of their teeth. Treat.
Introduce the finger brush with toothpaste. Let them lick and chew it.
Gently brush the outside of the front teeth for 5 seconds. Treat.
Gradually increase: canines, premolars, then back molars over 2 weeks.
success_criteria: {dog_name} allows 30 seconds of brushing on the outside of all teeth.
pro_tips: Only brush the OUTSIDE of the teeth — the tongue handles the inside.
The back molars are where tartar builds most. That's your target area.
common_mistakes: Using human toothpaste — fluoride is toxic to dogs.
Forcing the brush into a closed mouth — work on lip-lifting comfort first.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} won't let you near their mouth, go back to handling exercises. Practice lifting lips and touching gums with just your finger and treats for a week before introducing the brush.

title: Brush and Grooming Desensitization
overview: Teach {dog_name} to enjoy being brushed. Regular grooming prevents mats, distributes oils, and gives you a chance to check for skin issues, ticks, and bumps.
steps: Show {dog_name} the brush. Treat.
Touch the brush to their shoulder (easiest area). One stroke. Treat.
Gradually add strokes: 2, 3, 5, then continuous for 10 seconds.
Move to more sensitive areas: belly, legs, behind ears, tail.
Keep sessions short and positive — stop before {dog_name} gets restless.
success_criteria: {dog_name} tolerates 2 minutes of full-body brushing calmly.
pro_tips: Brush after exercise when {dog_name} is naturally calmer.
Match the brush to coat type: slicker for long coats, bristle for short, pin for wiry.
common_mistakes: Brushing too hard or pulling through mats — this hurts and creates brush-aversion.
Waiting until the coat is tangled to start brushing — prevention is easier than detangling.
troubleshooting: For mat-prone breeds, spritz with a detangling spray before brushing. Start from the ends of the fur and work toward the skin to avoid pulling.

title: Ear Cleaning Introduction
overview: Regular ear checks and cleaning prevent infections, especially in floppy-eared breeds. Teach {dog_name} to accept ear handling while they're young.
steps: Lift {dog_name}'s ear flap gently. Look inside. Treat.
Touch the outside of the ear canal with your finger. Treat.
Dampen a cotton ball with ear cleaner. Wipe the visible part of the inner ear.
If your vet recommends drops, practice the motion with treats first (tip head, pretend to squeeze).
Check ears weekly; clean monthly or as your vet advises.
success_criteria: {dog_name} allows both ears to be inspected and wiped without head-shaking or resistance.
pro_tips: Floppy-eared breeds need more frequent ear cleaning — moisture gets trapped.
Red, swollen, or smelly ears = vet visit. Don't try to treat infections yourself.
common_mistakes: Using Q-tips deep in the ear canal — cotton balls on the visible area only.
Skipping regular checks — catching problems early prevents costly vet bills.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} won't let you near their ears, they may have an existing infection or pain. Get a vet check first, then rebuild handling comfort once any medical issues are resolved.

title: Bath Time Desensitization
overview: Introduce {dog_name} to water and the bath experience gradually. A puppy who enjoys baths makes grooming a breeze for their entire life.
steps: Place a non-slip mat in the tub or sink. Let {dog_name} stand on it with no water. Treat.
Add 1 inch of lukewarm water. Let them stand in it. Treat.
Cup water and pour gently on their back (avoid head). Treat after each pour.
Apply a small amount of dog shampoo and massage gently. Treat.
Rinse thoroughly, wrap in a towel, and offer high-value treats.
success_criteria: {dog_name} stands calmly in the tub for a full 5-minute bath without panicking.
pro_tips: A lick mat with peanut butter stuck to the tub wall is a game-changer during baths.
Warm water, warm room. Cold baths create negative associations.
common_mistakes: Spraying water directly at {dog_name}'s face — use a wet cloth for the head area.
Skipping the non-slip mat — sliding in a tub is terrifying for puppies.
