troubleshooting: If {dog_name} panics in the tub, start with a damp sponge bath on the floor. Build up to standing in a shallow tub over 2-3 weeks.

title: Weight Check and Body Condition
overview: Learn to assess {dog_name}'s weight and body condition at home. Puppy weight matters — overweight puppies face joint problems, especially in large breeds.
steps: Weigh yourself on a bathroom scale. Then pick up {dog_name} and weigh together. Subtract.
Feel {dog_name}'s ribs: you should feel them easily with light pressure but not see them.
Look from above: there should be a visible waist (narrowing behind the ribs).
Look from the side: the belly should tuck up slightly behind the rib cage.
Record weight weekly in PupPal's health tracker. Compare to {breed} growth curves.
success_criteria: You can assess {dog_name}'s body condition score accurately and track weight weekly.
pro_tips: Large breed puppies should grow SLOWLY. Rapid weight gain increases hip and joint problems.
Use PupPal's breed growth curve to see if {dog_name} is on track.
common_mistakes: Judging weight by appearance alone — fluffy breeds hide extra weight under fur.
Overfeeding puppies because they always seem hungry — puppies are ALWAYS hungry.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} is gaining too fast (above breed curve), reduce meal portions by 10% and cut back on treat calories. Consult your vet for a specific feeding plan.

title: Eye Cleaning and Eye Drop Prep
overview: Many breeds need regular eye cleaning, and all dogs may need eye drops at some point. Teach {dog_name} to tolerate gentle eye handling now, before it's medically necessary.
steps: Hold a treat in one hand. With the other, gently touch {dog_name}'s cheek below the eye. Treat.
Progress to gently wiping below the eye with a soft cloth. Treat after each wipe.
Practice holding {dog_name}'s head still with one hand while wiping with the other. 3 seconds max, then treat.
Simulate eye drops: hold a small bottle above {dog_name}'s eye without dispensing anything. Treat for tolerance.
Drop a single drop of saline (not medicated) near the eye. Treat immediately.
Build to actual eye drop delivery if needed, always followed by high-value treats.
success_criteria: {dog_name} tolerates gentle eye wiping and simulated eye drops without head jerking or struggling.
pro_tips: Approach from the side, not straight on — it's less threatening. {breed_tip}
If {dog_name}'s breed is prone to tear staining, daily wiping prevents the discoloration.
Warm the saline slightly — cold drops are more startling.
common_mistakes: Forcing {dog_name}'s eyes open — we're not doing an exam. Just gentle surface handling.
Approaching from above and in front — this triggers a protective flinch in most dogs.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} absolutely won't tolerate face handling, go back to basics: treat for touching their chin, then cheek, then below the eye. Don't skip steps.

title: Medication Administration Practice
overview: Teach {dog_name} to accept pills, liquid medication, and paste from a syringe. Every dog needs medication at some point — training for it in advance removes the battle.
steps: Let {dog_name} lick peanut butter from the tip of an empty oral syringe. Make syringe = yummy.
Gently open {dog_name}'s mouth by tilting their head up. Squirt a tiny amount of water (or broth) from the syringe onto their tongue. Treat.
Practice hiding a treat inside a soft treat 'pocket.' Give it normally — {dog_name} swallows the whole thing.
Gently open {dog_name}'s mouth, place a treat on the back of their tongue, close, and stroke their throat downward. Treat after swallowing.
Practice the full sequence: open mouth, place 'pill' (use a treat), close, throat stroke, bonus treat.
Do this weekly so it's routine and stress-free if real medication is ever needed.
success_criteria: {dog_name} accepts mouth opening, simulated pill placement, and syringe delivery without struggling.
pro_tips: Real pill pockets (Greenies brand) work miracles for actual medication time. {breed_tip}
Never practice with real medication — only use treats and safe liquids during training.
This 2-minute weekly practice can save you massive stress during illness.
common_mistakes: Only practicing when {dog_name} is actually sick — by then they already feel bad and won't cooperate.
Forcing the mouth open aggressively — gentle head tilt and lip lift is all you need.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} clamps their mouth shut, smear peanut butter on your finger and let them lick it. While their mouth is open for licking, practice the gentle hold. Pair every mouth touch with food.

title: Wait for Food Bowl
overview: Teach {dog_name} that calm patience (not jumping and barking) is what makes good things happen. Meal time is the perfect daily practice opportunity.
steps: Prepare {dog_name}'s meal while they watch.
Hold the bowl at chest height. If {dog_name} jumps, raise it higher and wait.
When all four paws are on the ground (even briefly), begin lowering the bowl.
If they jump again, bowl goes up. When they're calm, bowl goes down.
Release with 'okay!' when the bowl reaches the floor.
success_criteria: {dog_name} sits or stands calmly while the bowl is placed on the floor, waiting for the release cue.
pro_tips: This is built-in twice-a-day training — no extra time needed.
Once mastered, increase wait time to 5 seconds, then 10, then 15.
common_mistakes: Making them wait too long and frustrating them — build gradually.
Not having a clear release cue — 'okay' should be consistent.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} breaks every time, lower the criteria — even 1 second of stillness earns the bowl. Build from there.

title: Door Manners
overview: Teach {dog_name} not to bolt through open doors. This is both a safety skill and an impulse control exercise that happens multiple times daily.
steps: Approach a door with {dog_name} on leash.
Begin opening the door. If {dog_name} rushes forward, close it (gently).
When {dog_name} pauses or looks at you, open it a crack. Close if they rush.
Continue until the door is fully open and {dog_name} is not lunging through.
Release with 'okay!' and walk through together.
success_criteria: {dog_name} waits at an open door until released 4 out of 5 times.
pro_tips: Practice at EVERY door, every time. Front door, back door, car door, crate door.
This is also a great visitor management tool — {dog_name} learns doors opening doesn't mean chaos.
common_mistakes: Slamming the door — close it calmly and quickly, just enough to block the rush.
Only practicing at the front door — consistency across all doors matters.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} is too excited at the front door, practice at boring interior doors first. Build the concept, then add the exciting doors.

title: No Jumping on People
overview: Jumping is one of the most common complaints from puppy owners. Teach {dog_name} that keeping four paws on the floor gets attention, and jumping makes attention disappear.
steps: When {dog_name} jumps on you, immediately turn your back. Cross your arms. Say nothing.
Wait for four paws on the floor. The INSTANT they land, turn back and calmly pet/treat.
If they jump again, back-turn again. Repeat.
Pre-empt jumping by asking for 'sit' before greetings. Reward the sit with attention.
Have friends and visitors do the same — consistency is critical.
success_criteria: {dog_name} greets people with four on the floor (or a sit) 7 out of 10 times.
pro_tips: Ignoring is the HARDEST part for humans. No 'down!' no pushing — any attention rewards jumping.
Keep {dog_name} on leash during greetings to prevent jumping on people who don't follow the rules.
common_mistakes: Pushing {dog_name} off — pushing is attention, which is what they want.
Inconsistency — one person allowing jumping undermines everyone's work.
troubleshooting: If jumping is extreme, put {dog_name} on a leash before greetings and step on the leash with enough slack to sit/stand but not jump. This prevents the behavior mechanically while you train the replacement.

title: Settle on Cue
overview: Teach {dog_name} an 'off switch' — the ability to go from active to calm on cue. This is essential for cafes, offices, and family movie night.
steps: After exercise (when {dog_name} is naturally calmer), sit in a chair with {dog_name} on leash.
Scatter a few treats on the ground near you. Then wait.
Mark and treat any calm behavior: lying down, resting head, sighing.
Ignore any attention-seeking: pawing, whining, pacing.
Gradually increase the duration. Introduce a chew toy for longer settles.
success_criteria: {dog_name} lies calmly near you for 15 minutes without demanding attention.
pro_tips: This is a 'capturing' exercise — you're rewarding a state of being, not an action.
Practice during dinner time (your dinner). {dog_name} on leash tied to a furniture leg, chew toy available.
common_mistakes: Practicing when {dog_name} is full of energy — settle after exercise, not instead of exercise.
Rewarding too enthusiastically — calm praise for calm behavior. Match their energy.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} can't settle at all, they're probably under-exercised or over-stimulated. Add a 15-minute play session before attempting settle practice.

title: Polite Greeting Protocol
overview: Combine sit, focus, and impulse control into a structured greeting routine. This replaces the chaos of meeting new people with a calm, predictable process.
steps: Have a helper approach from 20 feet away. Ask {dog_name} to sit.
If {dog_name} holds the sit, the helper takes 5 steps closer. If they break, helper stops.
Continue: calm = closer, excited = stop/backup.
When the helper reaches you and {dog_name} is sitting, the helper can pet calmly.
If {dog_name} jumps, helper immediately turns away. Reset to sit.
success_criteria: {dog_name} maintains a sit while a friendly stranger approaches and offers calm petting.
pro_tips: Practice with 5+ different people — {dog_name} needs to generalize the behavior.
Some dogs do better with a 'sit' and some with a 'four on the floor' — use whichever works for {dog_name}.
common_mistakes: Letting excited strangers rush up to pet {dog_name} — control the approach.
Rewarding the greeting with rough play — keep the energy low.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} can't sit at all when people approach, start with a person standing still 20 feet away and reward {dog_name} for looking at you instead of them. Build proximity tolerance gradually.

title: It's Yer Choice (Advanced Leave It)
overview: An advanced impulse control game where {dog_name} learns that ignoring temptation is what makes good things happen. This builds on basic leave it.
steps: Place a treat on the ground. Cover it with your hand if {dog_name} goes for it.
When they look away from the treat on the ground, mark "yes!" and give a DIFFERENT treat.
Gradually increase difficulty: remove your hand, step back, place higher-value items.
The rule: the item on the ground is NEVER the reward. Self-control earns something better.
Practice with toys, food, and novel items.
success_criteria: {dog_name} ignores an uncovered treat on the ground for 10 seconds without being told.
pro_tips: This game teaches {dog_name} to make good choices independently — not just when you give a cue.
Use this as a pre-walk warm-up to put {dog_name} in a focused, self-controlled mindset.
common_mistakes: Letting them eventually get the floor treat — the floor treat is ALWAYS off-limits.
Moving too fast to uncovered treats — build each step over multiple sessions.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} snatches the treat before you can cover it, start with the treat in your closed hand again. Only move to the floor when hand-based choices are 100%.

title: Doorbell Protocol
overview: The doorbell is the ultimate impulse control test. Teach {dog_name} that the doorbell means 'go to your spot and wait' instead of 'lose your mind and rush the door.'
steps: Place a mat or bed 6-8 feet from the front door. Practice 'place' on this mat first (no doorbell).
Have your helper ring the doorbell. Before {dog_name} reacts, toss a treat onto the mat.
If {dog_name} goes to the mat, rapid-fire treat. If they rush the door, calmly redirect to the mat.
Repeat 10 times: doorbell → treat on mat. {dog_name} starts anticipating mat instead of door.
Add a verbal cue: 'place!' right after the doorbell. Then treat on the mat.
Eventually answer the door with {dog_name} on the mat. Helper treats through the door crack for calm behavior.
success_criteria: {dog_name} goes to their mat within 5 seconds of the doorbell 6 out of 10 times.
pro_tips: Practice when no one is actually coming over — doorbell drills at random times.
{breed_tip}
This can take 2-3 weeks of daily practice. Don't expect overnight results.
common_mistakes: Only practicing when real guests arrive — too exciting. Practice with helpers on boring practice sessions.
Yelling 'no!' when {dog_name} barks — this adds to the chaos. Redirect silently to the mat.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} is too aroused by the doorbell to think, use a knock first (less exciting) and build up to the doorbell. Or change your doorbell sound to something less stimulating.

title: Wait Before Eating
overview: Expand {dog_name}'s food bowl wait to include waiting while you prepare the food, not just when you set it down. This builds patience and reduces meal-time frenzy.
steps: Begin preparing {dog_name}'s food. If they jump or bark, stop all preparation. Freeze. Wait for calm.
Resume preparation when {dog_name} is calm (sitting, standing quietly). If excitement returns, freeze again.
Once food is ready, cue 'sit' then 'wait.' Place the bowl down.
If {dog_name} breaks before your release word, pick the bowl back up. Recue 'sit' and 'wait.'
Release with 'okay!' or 'eat!' and let them enjoy.
Build the wait time from 3 seconds to 30 seconds over two weeks.
success_criteria: {dog_name} waits calmly during food preparation and holds a 15-second wait after the bowl is placed.
pro_tips: This exercise done twice daily (every meal) adds up fast. It's free training. {breed_tip}
Vary the wait time randomly — 5 seconds, then 20, then 8. Unpredictability builds stronger impulse control.
Never tease {dog_name} during the wait. This should feel fair, not frustrating.
common_mistakes: Only practicing the wait at bowl-down but not during preparation — both phases matter.
Making the wait so long that {dog_name} gives up and walks away. Keep it achievable.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} simply cannot sit still during food prep, have them wait in a sit behind a baby gate while you prepare. Release to eat when ready.

title: Ignore Dropped Food
overview: Teach {dog_name} that food falling on the floor doesn't mean free buffet. This prevents counter surfing behavior and keeps {dog_name} safe from eating harmful dropped items.
steps: With {dog_name} on leash (so you have control), drop a piece of kibble on the floor.
Cover it with your foot before {dog_name} can grab it. Say 'leave it.'
Wait for {dog_name} to look up at you. Mark 'yes!' and give a BETTER treat from your hand.
Repeat 10 times. {dog_name} learns: ignoring floor food = better food from you.
Gradually remove the foot cover — drop the kibble and just say 'leave it.'
Practice while cooking: accidentally 'drop' a piece of food. Reward {dog_name} for looking at you instead of grabbing it.
success_criteria: {dog_name} looks at you instead of lunging for dropped food 7 out of 10 times without foot coverage.
pro_tips: The kitchen is the final exam. Practice until {dog_name} ignores dropped food while you cook. {breed_tip}
This could save {dog_name}'s life — grapes, chocolate, xylitol gum on the sidewalk.
Practice with increasingly tempting items: kibble → cheese → chicken.
common_mistakes: Practicing only in one room — {dog_name} needs to generalize this to kitchen, yard, and walks.
Letting {dog_name} eat the floor food 'just this once' — one exception confuses the whole system.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} is too fast for your foot, toss the food farther away so you have time to step on it. Or use a leash for control.

title: Relaxation Protocol (Karen Overall)
overview: A structured program that teaches {dog_name} to remain calm and relaxed on a mat through increasingly challenging scenarios. This is the gold standard for teaching default calmness.
steps: Place {dog_name} on a mat and cue 'settle.' Treat for lying down calmly.
Day 1: Treat every 5 seconds of calm for 2 minutes. That's it.
Day 2: Stand up, sit back down. Treat for staying on mat.
Day 3: Take 1 step away, return. Treat. Take 2 steps. Treat.
Day 4: Clap once. Treat for staying. Stomp once. Treat for staying.
Day 5+: Gradually add more complex movements: jumping jacks, touching the door, opening the door, doorbell, dropping objects.
Always return to {dog_name} and treat ON the mat. The mat = the magic calm place.
success_criteria: {dog_name} remains on the mat through 15+ different distractions for a 10-minute session.
pro_tips: This protocol was designed by a veterinary behaviorist. It works for anxious dogs of all breeds. {breed_tip}
Go at {dog_name}'s pace — if they break, the distraction was too hard. Go back one step.
Eventually the mat becomes a portable 'calm switch' you can take anywhere.
common_mistakes: Skipping levels — the progression is designed in a specific order for a reason.
Treating too slowly — in the beginning, treat every few seconds. You can't over-reward calm behavior.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} won't stay on the mat at all, go back to basic 'settle' training. The mat needs to be a rewarding place before you add any distractions.

title: No Counter Surfing
overview: Teach {dog_name} that counters, tables, and elevated surfaces are never for them. Prevention now is infinitely easier than breaking a counter-surfing habit later.
steps: Place boring food on the counter edge where {dog_name} might reach it.
With {dog_name} on leash, walk near the counter. Watch their body language.
The instant {dog_name} looks at the counter food, say 'leave it' and reward from your hand when they look at you.
If {dog_name} jumps up, gently redirect with the leash and cue 'off.' Treat when all four paws are on the floor.
Practice 10 times per session. The message: counter food is invisible; your hand has better stuff.
Never leave food unattended on counters during the training period. Prevention is key.
success_criteria: {dog_name} walks past counter food without jumping up or fixating 8 out of 10 times.
pro_tips: One successful counter surf (they steal food) can undo weeks of training. Manage the environment. {breed_tip}
Push food away from counter edges when not actively training.
Baby gates to keep {dog_name} out of the kitchen are not cheating — they're smart management.
common_mistakes: Leaving food on counters and hoping {dog_name} won't notice — they will. Always will.
Only training 'off' after they've already surfed. Prevention is better than correction.
troubleshooting: If counter surfing is already established, management is your primary tool. Block kitchen access with gates and only allow supervised entry. Retrain from scratch.

title: Calm Greeting of Returning Owner
overview: Teach {dog_name} that your homecoming isn't a five-alarm event. Calm arrivals prevent jumping, over-arousal, and excitement peeing.
steps: When you arrive home, completely ignore {dog_name} for the first 30 seconds. No talking, no eye contact, no petting.
Put your things down. Take off your shoes. Act boring.
When {dog_name} offers ANY calm behavior (sits, stands still, four paws on floor), THEN greet them warmly.
If they jump, turn away silently. Wait for four-on-the-floor, then pet and greet.
Practice 'fake departures': leave for 10 seconds, return, practice the calm greeting. Repeat 5 times.
Over time, {dog_name} learns: owner home = wait calmly → get attention.
success_criteria: {dog_name} greets you with four paws on the floor within 15 seconds of your arrival.
pro_tips: This is HARD for humans. We want to greet our puppies enthusiastically. But calm arrivals prevent jumping long-term. {breed_tip}
All family members must follow the same protocol or it won't work.
Warn visitors in advance: 'Please ignore the puppy until they're calm.'
common_mistakes: Pushing {dog_name} down when they jump — pushing IS attention. Turn away instead.
Being calm on arrival but then getting super excited 5 seconds later — mixed signals.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} excitement-pees during greetings, keep arrivals extra boring and let them outside to potty before any greeting attention.

title: Harness Introduction
overview: Before leash training, {dog_name} needs to be comfortable wearing a harness. This short exercise builds positive associations with the gear.
steps: Show {dog_name} the harness and let them sniff it. Treat.
Touch the harness to their body. Treat.
Drape it over their back. Treat.
Clip it loosely. Treat. Let them walk around indoors.
Tighten to proper fit (two fingers between harness and body). Play and treat.
success_criteria: {dog_name} stands calmly while the harness is put on, with no freezing, biting, or avoidance.
pro_tips: A front-clip harness reduces pulling for most breeds. Back-clip for small breeds.
Put the harness on before meals so {dog_name} associates it with good things.
common_mistakes: Forcing the harness on — take it slow. Some puppies need 3-4 sessions.
Only putting the harness on for walks — this creates anticipation and excitement.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} freezes or refuses to walk in the harness, the fit may be wrong or it's too tight. Check for rubbing and adjust. Let them wear it indoors with play to build comfort.

title: Indoor Leash Walking
overview: Practice walking on a loose leash inside your home before adding the chaos of the outdoors. This is where {dog_name} learns the basic concept: loose leash = we move forward.
steps: Attach leash to harness. Stand still and wait.
When {dog_name} looks at you or the leash goes slack, mark 'yes!' and take a step forward.
If they pull ahead, stop immediately. Become a tree. Wait for slack.
The instant the leash relaxes, mark and move forward again.
Walk around your home for 3-5 minutes, rewarding every few steps of loose leash.
success_criteria: {dog_name} walks through 2 rooms on a loose leash with fewer than 3 stops.
pro_tips: Hold treats in your leash-side hand. This naturally keeps {dog_name} near your side.
Change direction often — this teaches {dog_name} to pay attention to where YOU'RE going.
common_mistakes: Walking forward when the leash is tight — this rewards pulling.
Jerking the leash — use stopping power, not corrections.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} sits and won't move, the leash may be intimidating. Drop the leash and just walk — let them follow you freely first.

title: Loose Leash Walking Outdoors
overview: Transfer indoor skills to the real world. Outdoors is MUCH harder — every smell, sound, and sight competes for {dog_name}'s attention.
steps: Start in a low-distraction outdoor area (quiet street, backyard).
Use the same rules: loose leash = forward, tight leash = stop.
Reward frequently — every 3-5 steps at first. This rate is MUCH higher than indoors.
Allow sniff breaks as rewards: 'go sniff!' then back to walking.
Gradually reduce treat frequency as {dog_name} improves.
success_criteria: {dog_name} walks 50 feet on a loose leash outdoors with 5 or fewer stops.
pro_tips: Structured sniff breaks reduce pulling more than any technique. Give {dog_name} 30-second 'go sniff' breaks every few minutes.
Walk before feeding — a hungry {dog_name} is more treat-motivated.
common_mistakes: Expecting outdoor behavior to match indoor behavior immediately.
Marathon walks too early — 10-15 minute practice walks beat 45-minute pulling walks.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} pulls constantly outdoors, reduce the challenge: shorter walks, quieter areas, better treats. Build success, don't fight failure.

title: Direction Changes
overview: Teach {dog_name} to follow your direction changes smoothly. This keeps them engaged and paying attention on walks instead of going on autopilot.
steps: While walking, make a 180-degree turn without warning. Pat your leg to encourage {dog_name} to follow.
The instant they catch up and match your pace, mark 'yes!' and treat.
Practice left turns (into {dog_name}'s path — they slow down), right turns (away — they speed up), and U-turns.
Keep sessions unpredictable — {dog_name} should never know which direction comes next.
Gradually reduce treats to every 3rd successful turn.
success_criteria: {dog_name} follows 3 consecutive direction changes without the leash going tight.
pro_tips: Turn INTO {dog_name} occasionally to gently body-block them — they learn to give you space.
Make your body language clear: look where you're going, not at your dog.
common_mistakes: Jerking the leash during turns — just change direction; let {dog_name} figure it out.
Always walking the same route — variety is the enemy of autopilot walking.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} doesn't notice direction changes, you may need to be more dramatic. Try jogging a few steps in the new direction or making a kissy sound.

title: Walking Past Distractions
overview: Teach {dog_name} to maintain loose leash walking when they see something exciting — other dogs, people, squirrels. This is where real-world manners begin.
steps: Start at a distance where {dog_name} can SEE the distraction but isn't losing their mind.
Mark and treat any eye contact or loose leash. Rain treats for focus near distractions.
Gradually decrease distance over multiple sessions.
If {dog_name} locks onto the distraction and won't take treats, you're TOO CLOSE. Increase distance.
Use 'look at me' or 'let's go' as you pass distractions.
success_criteria: {dog_name} walks past another dog at 10 feet without pulling or lunging.
pro_tips: The threshold distance (where {dog_name} can still think and eat) is your starting point. Work from there.
Don't force face-to-face greetings on leash — this creates leash frustration.
common_mistakes: Getting too close too fast — slow systematic desensitization works; flooding doesn't.
Tightening the leash when you see a distraction — {dog_name} reads your tension.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} is reactive to other dogs on leash, this exercise alone may not be enough. Consider working with a certified trainer (CPDT-KA) for a custom behavior modification plan.

title: Heel Position Introduction
overview: Teach {dog_name} a formal heel — walking at your left side with attention. This is not for everyday walks (which should include sniff breaks) but for situations requiring precise control.
steps: Hold treats in your left hand at your side.
Lure {dog_name} to your left side, facing forward. Mark and treat.
Take one step. If {dog_name} stays at your side, mark and treat.
Build to 3 steps, then 5, then 10 in heel position.
Add the cue "heel" when they reliably walk in position.
success_criteria: {dog_name} walks in heel position for 20 steps with attention.
pro_tips: Heel is for crossing busy streets, passing other dogs, and entering buildings. Not for the entire walk.
Alternate between heel and loose leash walking — heel is mentally tiring.
common_mistakes: Expecting heel for entire walks — this is exhausting and unfair. Sniff time matters.
Treating when {dog_name} is ahead of or behind you — precision matters here.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} forges ahead during heel, turn into them (left turn) to reset their position. Reward when they are back at your side.

title: Stop-and-Go Leash Protocol
overview: Teach {dog_name} that pulling makes the walk stop, and loose leash makes it continue. This is the most effective anti-pulling technique for {breed} puppies.
steps: Start walking with {dog_name} on a loose leash.
The instant the leash goes taut, stop completely. Plant your feet. Say nothing.
Wait. {dog_name} will eventually look back at you or take a step back, creating slack.
The moment the leash is loose, mark 'yes!' and start walking again. Walking is the reward.
Every 10-15 steps of nice loose leash, also deliver a treat at your side.
If {dog_name} is very excited, start this exercise in your driveway or backyard, not on a real walk.
success_criteria: Walking 30+ steps consecutively with a loose leash before needing to stop.
pro_tips: The first 5-10 sessions will be SLOW. You might cover 50 feet in 15 minutes. That's normal.
{breed_tip}
Consistency is everything — if you stop for pulling sometimes but not others, {dog_name} learns to keep trying.
common_mistakes: Yanking the leash back instead of planting your feet — jerking teaches {dog_name} nothing except that you're unpredictable.
Giving up and letting {dog_name} pull to get to the park — one exception erases ten good sessions.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} doesn't look back at you when you stop, try making a kissy noise once. If they turn, mark and move. Gradually fade the noise.

title: Penalty Yards (Reverse Direction)
overview: When {dog_name} pulls toward something, turn and walk the opposite direction. They learn that pulling AWAY from the goal is what pulling creates.
steps: Walk toward a destination {dog_name} wants (the park, another dog, etc.).
When {dog_name} pulls, say 'oops!' cheerfully and turn 180 degrees. Walk the other direction.
When the leash is loose and {dog_name} catches up, mark 'yes!' and turn back toward the goal.
If they pull again, turn again. You might 'yo-yo' back and forth many times initially.
Eventually {dog_name} learns: the fastest way to GET there is to walk nicely.
Celebrate when {dog_name} walks past a trigger without pulling — big treat and praise.
success_criteria: {dog_name} approaches a desired destination (park, fire hydrant, etc.) with a loose leash 6 out of 10 approaches.
pro_tips: Pick a short walk with a clear 'goal' destination at first — the mailbox, a specific tree.
{breed_tip}
Don't get frustrated. Think of it as a game: how quickly will {dog_name} figure out the puzzle?
common_mistakes: Jerking {dog_name} when turning — just turn smoothly. They'll follow.
Only using this near high-excitement triggers. Practice on boring walks too.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} anchors and won't turn with you, use a treat to lure the first few turns. Fade the lure after they understand the game.

title: Leash Reactivity Prevention
overview: Teach {dog_name} to look at you when they see another dog on leash, instead of lunging, barking, or fixating. Prevention during puppyhood is 10x easier than rehabilitation later.
steps: When you spot another dog in the distance (before {dog_name} reacts), say '{dog_name}, look!' and hold a treat at your eyes.
The moment {dog_name} looks at you, mark 'yes!' and feed 3-4 treats rapidly.
Keep treating as the other dog passes. You want {dog_name} to associate other dogs with a treat party.
Gradually decrease distance to other dogs over weeks, always staying below {dog_name}'s reaction threshold.
If {dog_name} stares, hackles up, or lunges — you're too close. Increase distance immediately.
The goal is: see dog → look at owner. This is called a 'conditioned emotional response.'
success_criteria: {dog_name} auto-orients to you when spotting another dog at 20+ feet 7 out of 10 times.
pro_tips: Use real chicken, hot dogs, or cheese — this exercise needs your best treats.
{breed_tip}
Never let another dog approach on leash without your permission. It's okay to say 'we're in training, sorry!'
common_mistakes: Tightening the leash when you see another dog — your tension travels straight to {dog_name}.
Forcing {dog_name} to 'say hi' to dogs they're uncomfortable with. Distance is your friend.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} is already fixating and won't take treats, you're way too close. Create distance until {dog_name} can eat again, then work from there.

title: Urban Walking Skills
overview: Prepare {dog_name} for walking in busy environments: sidewalks, crosswalks, street noise, crowds. Build confidence alongside leash manners.
steps: Start on a quiet side street during a low-traffic time.
Walk at {dog_name}'s pace. Treat frequently for staying near you and not bolting.
Practice sitting at every curb before crossing — this is a safety behavior.
When a loud vehicle passes, scatter treats on the ground to create a positive association with noise.
Practice walking past storefronts, parked cars, and garbage cans — all novel objects for a puppy.
Keep initial urban walks to 10-15 minutes. End while {dog_name} is still confident, not overwhelmed.
success_criteria: {dog_name} walks on a busy sidewalk for 10 minutes without panicking, bolting, or shutting down.
pro_tips: Go during quieter hours first (early morning, mid-afternoon) and work up to busier times.
{breed_tip}
Let {dog_name} sniff — urban walks should be 50% walk, 50% investigate. Sniffing is calming.
common_mistakes: Overwhelming {dog_name} with a busy downtown walk on the first try. Gradual exposure is key.
Dragging {dog_name} past scary things. Let them observe from a comfortable distance.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} freezes or tries to bolt, don't force movement. Sit on a bench, let them observe from safety, feed treats for calm behavior. Try again another day at lower intensity.

title: Leash Manners at the Vet
overview: Vet lobbies are full of triggers: other animals, strange smells, anxious energy. Train {dog_name} to stay calm on leash in this specific high-stress environment.
steps: Visit your vet's parking lot first. Just sit in the car with {dog_name} and treat for calm behavior.
On the next visit, walk into the lobby, treat heavily, and leave after 2 minutes. No appointment.
On the third visit, practice 'sit' and 'settle' on a mat in the lobby. Treat continuously for calm.
Gradually increase lobby time to 10 minutes. Practice ignoring other animals.
Ask vet staff if {dog_name} can have treats on the exam table. Create positive associations with the clinic.
On actual appointment days, arrive early to practice calm lobby behavior first.
success_criteria: {dog_name} sits calmly on a mat in the vet lobby for 5 minutes without lunging at other animals.
pro_tips: Many vets allow 'happy visits' — quick lobby trips with treats and no exam. Ask yours.
{breed_tip}
Bring {dog_name}'s favorite mat — familiar scent reduces stress in unfamiliar environments.
common_mistakes: Only visiting the vet for shots and exams — {dog_name} learns vet = bad things happen.
Comforting a stressed {dog_name} with baby talk — this often reinforces the anxiety.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} is too stressed to eat in the vet lobby, you need more parking lot visits first. When they eat freely in the lot, try the lobby.

title: Sniff Walk (Decompression Walk)
overview: Not every walk needs to be training. Teach {dog_name} 'go sniff' as a release cue for enrichment walks where they lead and you follow. This reduces frustration and improves leash skills overall.
steps: Use a longer leash (10-15 feet) or a standard leash at full extension.
Say 'go sniff!' and let {dog_name} explore at their own pace. Follow them.
Don't redirect, don't ask for commands, don't rush. This is {dog_name}'s time.
If {dog_name} pulls past the leash length, just stop and wait. Don't correct.
After 15 minutes, switch to structured walk mode with a normal leash if desired.
Aim for 2-3 sniff walks per week in addition to regular training walks.
success_criteria: {dog_name} sniffs and explores calmly on a long leash without excessive pulling for 15 minutes.
pro_tips: 20 minutes of sniffing tires a puppy as much as 40 minutes of walking. Sniffing IS exercise.
{breed_tip}
Sniff walks reduce overall leash frustration, which improves behavior on structured walks.
common_mistakes: Micromanaging a sniff walk — the whole point is to let {dog_name} choose.
Only doing structured walks — puppies NEED unstructured exploration time.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} eats everything they sniff, practice 'leave it' on structured walks first. Sniff walks work best when {dog_name} has basic impulse control around ground items.

title: Muffin Tin Puzzle
overview: A simple DIY puzzle that makes {dog_name} use their brain to find hidden treats. Mental exercise tires puppies faster than physical exercise.
steps: Place treats in 3-4 cups of a muffin tin.
Cover ALL cups with tennis balls (treats and empty ones).
Let {dog_name} figure out how to remove the balls to find the treats.
Start with some cups uncovered so they understand the concept.
Increase difficulty by putting fewer treats in more cups.
success_criteria: {dog_name} clears all balls and finds all treats within 3 minutes.
pro_tips: This is a great meal replacement — put their entire breakfast in the muffin tin.
Rotate puzzle types weekly. Novelty is what makes puzzles challenging.
common_mistakes: Making it too hard initially — success should come easily at first.
Only using commercial puzzle toys — DIY works just as well.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} gives up or gets frustrated, start with no balls at all (treats visible). Then cover one cup. Build difficulty gradually.

title: Nose Work Introduction
overview: Teach {dog_name} to use their most powerful sense. Scent work builds confidence, provides mental exhaustion, and is accessible to dogs of all ages and abilities.
steps: Let {dog_name} watch you place a treat under one of 3 overturned cups.
Release them to find it. Mark and let them eat when they nose the right cup.
Once reliable, place the treat WITHOUT {dog_name} watching.
Let them use their nose to find it. Celebrate when they do.
Increase difficulty: more cups, farther apart, different rooms.
success_criteria: {dog_name} can find a hidden treat under 1 of 5 cups without seeing where it was placed.
pro_tips: Use smelly treats (freeze-dried liver, cheese) for scent work — they need to smell, not see.
10 minutes of nose work = 30 minutes of physical exercise in terms of mental tiredness.
common_mistakes: Helping too much — let {dog_name} problem-solve. Struggling is part of learning.
Using non-smelly treats — kibble is too subtle for beginning scent work.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} just knocks over all cups randomly, start with only one cup. One cup = one treat. Build from there.

title: Hide and Seek
overview: Combine recall practice with play. {dog_name} searches the house for you, strengthening their bond and their desire to find and check in with you.
steps: Have someone hold {dog_name} or ask for a 'wait.'
Hide behind a door or furniture in another room.
Call '{dog_name}, come find me!' in an excited voice.
When they find you, throw a party — treats, play, excitement.
Start with easy hiding spots and progress to harder ones.
success_criteria: {dog_name} actively searches through 3+ rooms to find you when called.
pro_tips: This game strengthens recall in a low-pressure way. {dog_name} learns that finding you = best thing ever.
Kids love playing this too — it builds the dog-child bond.
common_mistakes: Hiding too well initially — they need early wins to stay motivated.
Not rewarding enthusiastically — the find should be EXCITING.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} gives up searching, make a sound to help them. As they get better, make your hiding spots more creative.

title: Snuffle Mat Feeding
overview: Replace the food bowl with a snuffle mat to make mealtime a 10-15 minute enrichment activity instead of 30 seconds of scarfing.
steps: Spread {dog_name}'s meal across the snuffle mat (fleece strips hide the food).
Place the mat on the ground and let {dog_name} sniff and forage.
Supervise to prevent chewing the mat itself.
Start with food more visible, gradually bury it deeper.
Use for 1-2 meals per week as enrichment rotation.
success_criteria: {dog_name} eats their full meal from the snuffle mat, taking 10+ minutes instead of under 1 minute from a bowl.
pro_tips: Snuffle mats are great for anxious puppies — sniffing is naturally calming.
Wash the mat weekly in a laundry bag.
common_mistakes: Leaving {dog_name} unsupervised — some puppies chew and ingest the fabric.
Using every single meal — variety keeps enrichment interesting.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} flips the mat, place it on a non-slip surface or inside a box to keep it in place.

title: Scatter Feeding in the Yard
overview: Replace the food bowl by scattering {dog_name}'s entire meal across the yard or a room. This activates their natural foraging instinct and provides 15-20 minutes of mental exercise.
steps: Take {dog_name}'s full meal portion outside (or to a room with short carpet).
Scatter kibble across a 10x10 foot area in the grass.
Release {dog_name} with "find it!" and let them forage.
Start with a small area and high visibility, then scatter wider and in taller grass.
Alternate between scatter feeding, snuffle mats, and puzzle toys throughout the week.
success_criteria: {dog_name} searches and finds all scattered kibble within 15 minutes.
pro_tips: Grass scatter feeding is the easiest enrichment — zero setup, zero cleanup.
This is especially calming for anxious puppies. Sniffing lowers heart rate and cortisol.
common_mistakes: Scattering in areas with pesticides or chemicals — only use safe, clean areas.
Doing it every single meal — variety matters. Alternate enrichment methods.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} gives up before finding all the food, start with a smaller scatter area. Success builds persistence.

title: Frozen Kong Recipes
overview: A frozen stuffed Kong is the Swiss Army knife of puppy enrichment. It soothes teething gums, provides mental stimulation, and buys you 20-30 minutes of quiet.
steps: Layer 1 (bottom): plug the small hole with peanut butter or cream cheese.
Layer 2 (middle): mix kibble with plain yogurt or mashed banana and fill 2/3.
Layer 3 (top): add small training treats or blueberries.
Seal the top with a thin layer of peanut butter.
Freeze for 4+ hours. Give during crate time, settle practice, or when you need quiet time.
success_criteria: {dog_name} works on the frozen Kong for 15+ minutes without losing interest.
pro_tips: Make 5 Kongs on Sunday night and freeze for the week. Always have one ready.
Difficulty levels: unfrozen (easy) → partially frozen (medium) → fully frozen (hard) → frozen with kibble plug at top (expert).
common_mistakes: Using ingredients that upset puppy stomachs — introduce new fillings one at a time.
Giving Kongs only in the crate — they are great in any calm-behavior context.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} gives up on a fully frozen Kong, start with unfrozen filling. As they get better, freeze for longer.

title: Shell Game
overview: The classic shell game adapted for {dog_name}. Hide a treat under one of three cups, shuffle, and let {dog_name} use their nose to find it. Great problem-solving exercise.
steps: Place 3 upside-down cups in a row. Show {dog_name} you're putting a treat under one.
Let {dog_name} knock over cups to find the treat. Mark 'yes!' when they find it.
Repeat 5 times with {dog_name} watching the placement.
Now swap the cups once after placing the treat. Can {dog_name} still find it?
Add more swaps. Most dogs track the cup — some use their nose instead. Both are valid.
Increase difficulty by using cups with less scent permeability or adding more cups.
success_criteria: {dog_name} correctly identifies the treat cup 7 out of 10 times with one shuffle.
pro_tips: This tests both problem-solving and nose work. Let {dog_name} use whichever strategy they prefer. {breed_tip}
Heavy ceramic bowls work better than light plastic cups that {dog_name} can flip accidentally.
This is great entertainment for visitors — show off {dog_name}'s detective skills.
common_mistakes: Punishing wrong guesses — wrong cup = no treat. That's enough feedback. Keep it fun.
Only using sight-based tracking. If {dog_name} uses their nose, that's even more impressive.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} just knocks over all three cups, use cups they can nose-point at but can't knock over. Reward pointing at the correct cup.

title: Box Shredding Party
overview: Give {dog_name} a cardboard box filled with treats and paper. Shredding is natural and satisfying — channeling it into an approved activity prevents destructive shredding elsewhere.
steps: Fill a small cardboard box with crumpled newspaper or tissue paper.
Scatter treats throughout the paper. Close the box loosely (don't tape it).
Present the box to {dog_name}. Let them figure out how to get the treats.
Allow shredding, nosing, and problem-solving. Supervise to prevent paper eating.
Once treats are found, clean up the cardboard. Don't leave it as a long-term chew.
Increase difficulty: tape the box lightly, nest boxes inside each other, use multiple small boxes.
success_criteria: {dog_name} gets all treats out of the box within 5 minutes and shows excitement for the activity.
pro_tips: Amazon boxes are perfect for this. Free enrichment. {breed_tip}
Supervise the entire time — some puppies eat cardboard, which can cause blockages.
This is a great 'rainy day' or 'work from home' activity — 10 minutes of busy puppy while you're on calls.
common_mistakes: Leaving {dog_name} unsupervised with cardboard — always supervise to prevent ingestion.
Using boxes with tape, staples, or packaging materials — these are hazards.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} eats the cardboard instead of shredding it, this activity isn't for them right now. Stick to food puzzles and Kongs.

title: Establish the Potty Schedule
overview: Build a consistent outdoor routine that teaches {dog_name} when and where to go. Puppies can hold it roughly 1 hour per month of age, so a young {breed} needs frequent trips outside.
steps: Take {dog_name} outside first thing in the morning, right to your designated potty spot.
Stand quietly and wait up to 3 minutes. Don't play or talk — this is business time.
The instant {dog_name} starts to go, say your cue word (e.g., 'go potty') in a calm voice.
As soon as they finish, mark with 'yes!' and give a high-value treat within 2 seconds.
Repeat after every meal, nap, play session, and before bed.
success_criteria: {dog_name} goes within 2 minutes of arriving at the potty spot 4 out of 5 times.
pro_tips: Keep a log for the first week — you'll find {dog_name}'s natural rhythm quickly.
{breed_tip}
Puppies often need to go 5-10 minutes after eating. Set a timer.
common_mistakes: Punishing accidents — this teaches {dog_name} to hide, not to hold it.
Waiting too long to treat — the reward must come within seconds of the behavior.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} won't go outside but goes immediately back indoors, stay outside longer (up to 10 min) and go to a spot where they've gone before. The scent helps trigger the behavior.

title: Introduce the Cue Word
overview: Pair a verbal cue with the act of eliminating so {dog_name} learns to go on command. This is invaluable for travel, vet visits, and rainy days.
steps: As {dog_name} begins to squat or lift a leg, calmly say your cue word once.
Let them finish completely without interruption.
Mark with 'yes!' and reward immediately.
Practice this pairing for 3-5 days before testing — say the cue BEFORE they start, then wait.
success_criteria: {dog_name} begins eliminating within 30 seconds of hearing the cue word in a familiar location.
pro_tips: Pick a cue that won't embarrass you in public. 'Go potty' or 'hurry up' work well.
Use the same cue for both #1 and #2 to keep it simple.
common_mistakes: Repeating the cue word over and over — say it once and wait.
Using the cue before the puppy has a reliable association (too early).
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} doesn't respond to the cue after a week, go back to just pairing the word WITH the behavior for 5 more days. Some puppies need more repetitions.

title: Accident Protocol
overview: Accidents will happen. How you respond makes the difference between a setback and a learning moment for {dog_name}.
steps: If you catch {dog_name} mid-accident, calmly interrupt with 'oops!' and immediately take them outside.
If they finish outside, reward as normal.
Clean the indoor spot with enzyme cleaner (NOT ammonia-based products).
Note the time and what happened before — were they unsupervised? Did you miss a schedule trip?
success_criteria: You reduce accidents to fewer than 2 per week within the first 10 days of consistent scheduling.
pro_tips: Enzyme cleaners break down the scent markers that tell {dog_name} 'this is a bathroom spot.'
If accidents spike, shrink {dog_name}'s unsupervised area. More freedom comes with more reliability.
common_mistakes: Rubbing their nose in it — this is punishment-based and damages trust.
Using vinegar or bleach — these don't fully eliminate scent markers.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} keeps going in the same indoor spot despite cleaning, block access to that area temporarily and supervise more closely. You may need a stronger enzyme cleaner.

title: Bell Training for Door Alerts
overview: Teach {dog_name} to ring a bell by the door when they need to go outside. This communication tool prevents accidents and builds independence.
steps: Hang bells at {dog_name}'s nose height on the door you use for potty trips.
Before every potty trip, hold a treat near the bells so {dog_name} touches them with their nose.
The instant the bells make sound, say 'yes!' and open the door.
After 3-5 days, wait for {dog_name} to touch the bells without luring.
Once they touch bells independently, ALWAYS open the door — even if it's a false alarm.
success_criteria: {dog_name} independently rings the bell before 3 out of 5 potty trips.
pro_tips: Use jingle bells or a commercial dog doorbell — both work fine.
If {dog_name} starts ringing for fun, only let them out briefly with no play. Ring = potty only.
common_mistakes: Ignoring the bell ring — this breaks the association fast.
Playing outside after bell ringing — keeps it strictly potty-related at first.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} is scared of the bell sound, start with something quieter like a touch button that beeps. Desensitize gradually.

title: Nighttime Potty Routine
overview: Help {dog_name} sleep through the night without accidents. Young puppies will need nighttime trips, but with the right routine, they'll drop them naturally.
steps: Pick up water 2 hours before bedtime.
Take {dog_name} out for a final potty trip right before bed.
Set an alarm for 3-4 hours into the night (adjust based on age).
Take them out silently — no play, no excitement. Potty only.
Gradually extend the alarm by 30 minutes every 3-4 nights as {dog_name} succeeds.
success_criteria: {dog_name} sleeps 6+ hours without needing a nighttime trip.
pro_tips: A crate next to your bed helps — {dog_name} will whine to alert you before going.
Most puppies can sleep through the night by 16 weeks. Be patient.
common_mistakes: Making the nighttime trip exciting — keep lights dim, voice low, no play.
Waiting for crying before taking them out — preempt with the alarm.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} cries at night after you've eliminated the need for a potty break, wait 30 seconds of silence before responding. Don't reward crying with attention.

title: Supervised Freedom Expansion
overview: Gradually give {dog_name} more unsupervised house access as potty reliability improves. This prevents regression from too much freedom too soon.
steps: Start with {dog_name} having access to only 1-2 rooms while supervised.
After 2 accident-free weeks in that area, open access to one more room.
Continue expanding every 2 weeks of zero accidents.
If an accident occurs, go back one level for another week.
By 6 months, most puppies can have full house access with supervision.
success_criteria: {dog_name} has access to 3+ rooms with zero accidents for 2 consecutive weeks.
pro_tips: Baby gates are your best investment during potty training.
Tether {dog_name} to you with a leash indoors if gates aren't practical — this prevents sneaking off.
common_mistakes: Giving full house access on day one — this guarantees hidden accidents.
Not supervising in new rooms — each new space needs active monitoring.
troubleshooting: If regression happens in a new room, {dog_name} may have found a hidden accident spot. Deep clean the entire room with enzyme cleaner and restart supervised access.

title: Apartment Potty Pad Transition
overview: For apartment-living {breed} owners, teach {dog_name} to use a potty pad initially, then transition to outdoor elimination as vaccination schedule allows.
steps: Place a potty pad near the door you will use for outdoor trips.
Take {dog_name} to the pad on the same schedule as outdoor potty training (after meals, naps, play).
Mark and treat when they use the pad correctly.
Once outdoor access is safe, move the pad closer to the door, then just outside, then remove it.
Transition over 1-2 weeks, not overnight.
success_criteria: {dog_name} uses the pad consistently, then transitions to outdoor elimination within 2 weeks.
pro_tips: Use grass-scented pads if you plan to transition outdoors — the scent bridges the gap.
Put the pad on a tray for easy cleanup.
common_mistakes: Using pads permanently when outdoor access is available — this can delay full house-training.
Moving the pad too fast — inch it toward the door over days.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} shreds the pads, try a grass patch system instead. Some puppies see pads as toys.

title: Regression Recovery Protocol
overview: Potty regression is normal — it happens during teething, growth spurts, schedule changes, and adolescence. Here is how to get {dog_name} back on track fast.
steps: Go back to the original schedule: outside every 1-2 hours.
Reduce unsupervised access — back to baby gates and tethering.
Reward every successful outdoor potty like it is the first time.
Deep clean all indoor accident spots with enzyme cleaner.
Maintain the stricter schedule for 1 week, then gradually relax again.
success_criteria: {dog_name} returns to previous reliability within 7-10 days of the reset.
pro_tips: Regression during adolescence (6-12 months) is extremely common. Do not panic.
If regression comes with increased thirst or frequency, see the vet — it could be a UTI.
common_mistakes: Assuming {dog_name} is being spiteful — dogs do not potty indoors out of revenge.
Giving up on the training — regression is a blip, not a restart.
troubleshooting: If regression lasts more than 2 weeks despite a schedule reset, consult your vet to rule out urinary tract infections or other medical causes.

title: Umbilical Cord Method
overview: Keep {dog_name} tethered to you with a leash indoors so you catch every pre-potty signal in real time. This prevents unsupervised accidents and speeds up housetraining dramatically for {breed} puppies.
steps: Clip {dog_name}'s leash to your belt loop or waist clip so both hands are free.
Go about your normal routine — {dog_name} follows you everywhere.
Watch for pre-potty signals: sniffing circles, whining, squatting, heading toward the door.
The moment you spot a signal, say 'outside!' and calmly walk {dog_name} to the potty spot.
Reward immediately after they go outside. Return indoors and reattach.
Practice for 30-60 minute stretches, alternating with crate or pen time.
success_criteria: {dog_name} has zero unsupervised accidents during umbilical sessions for 3 consecutive days.
pro_tips: This method works especially well in the first 2 weeks. {breed_tip}
Keep treats in a pouch on your hip so you're always ready to reward.
If you can't actively supervise, crate or pen — there's no middle ground during housetraining.
common_mistakes: Using a retractable leash — too much slack. Use a 4-6 foot standard leash.
Punishing when you miss a signal — the mistake was yours, not {dog_name}'s.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} fights the tether, let them drag the leash freely first for a day so they get used to it, then attach to your belt.

title: Potty on Cue in New Locations
overview: Generalize {dog_name}'s potty cue so it works anywhere — not just your backyard. Essential for travel, visits, and real-world reliability.
steps: Take {dog_name} to a new outdoor location they haven't pottied at before.
Walk to a grassy or appropriate area and stand still.
Give your established cue word ('go potty') and wait up to 5 minutes.
Mark and reward the instant {dog_name} goes. Celebrate big — this is harder than it seems.
Repeat in 3-5 different locations over the next week: friend's yard, park, sidewalk strip, hotel grounds.
success_criteria: {dog_name} responds to the potty cue in at least 3 different locations within 3 minutes.
pro_tips: Bring a paper towel with your yard's scent if {dog_name} is really struggling in new places.
{breed_tip}
Morning outings work best — {dog_name} has a full bladder and is more likely to succeed.
common_mistakes: Rushing it — some puppies need 5+ minutes to feel comfortable enough to go in a new spot.
Playing before potty — always business first, then play as the reward.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} absolutely won't go in a new place, walk briskly for 2-3 minutes to stimulate their system, then try the cue again while standing still.

title: Potty Training in Bad Weather
overview: Rain, snow, or cold shouldn't derail {dog_name}'s routine. Teach {dog_name} that potty time happens regardless of weather conditions.
steps: When weather is bad, take {dog_name} out on leash to the closest appropriate spot.
Use your potty cue immediately — no wandering or sniffing for fun.
Stay upbeat. If you act miserable, {dog_name} picks up on that energy.
The moment they go, give a high-value treat (something better than usual) and head back inside.
Dry {dog_name} off with the towel — make coming-back-inside feel cozy and rewarding.
success_criteria: {dog_name} potties within 3 minutes in rain or cold without refusing to go outside.
pro_tips: Keep a 'rainy day treat' that's extra special — only used for weather potty trips.
{breed_tip}
A covered porch potty area can be a lifesaver for tiny puppies in extreme weather.
common_mistakes: Carrying {dog_name} outside then putting them down — they need to walk out themselves to build the habit.
Giving up after 1 minute — some puppies need time to adjust to the sensation.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} flatly refuses to go outside in rain, try putting on a puppy raincoat first, or create a small sheltered potty area near your door.

title: Phasing Out Treats for Potty
overview: Gradually transition {dog_name} from treat-every-time to verbal praise only. By now the habit should be strong enough that the behavior is self-reinforcing.
steps: For the first 3 days, treat every other successful potty trip instead of every one. Always praise verbally.
Next 3 days, treat every third successful trip. Keep verbal praise enthusiastic every time.
By week 2, switch to random intermittent treats — maybe 1 in 5 trips. Praise stays consistent.
After a month of reliability, treats become occasional surprises. Verbal praise is the standard reward.
If {dog_name} regresses at any point, go back to treating every time for a few days.
success_criteria: {dog_name} potties reliably on cue with verbal praise only, no treat regression for 2 weeks.
pro_tips: Intermittent reinforcement is actually stronger than constant reinforcement — it's the slot machine effect.
{breed_tip}
Never fully stop treats — occasional surprise rewards keep the behavior strong long-term.
common_mistakes: Stopping treats cold turkey — always fade gradually.
Forgetting verbal praise — {dog_name} still needs acknowledgment even without treats.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} starts having accidents after reducing treats, it's too soon. Go back to full treat reinforcement for another week, then fade more slowly.

title: Vet Visit Preparation
overview: Make vet visits stress-free for {dog_name} by simulating the experience at home and doing 'happy visits' to the actual clinic.
steps: At home: practice handling all body parts (ears, mouth, paws, belly) with treats.
Simulate a scale: have {dog_name} stand on a flat surface or bathmat and reward stillness.
Practice being lifted onto surfaces (table, counter with towel for grip).
Visit the vet clinic just to get treats from the receptionist. No exam. Leave happy.
Do 3-4 'happy visits' before any actual appointments.
success_criteria: {dog_name} walks into the vet clinic without cowering, and accepts handling on an exam table with treats.
pro_tips: Ask your vet about 'fear free' practices — many modern clinics use treat-based handling.
Bring {dog_name}'s favorite treats to actual appointments. High-value only.
common_mistakes: Only going to the vet for shots — this creates negative associations.
Cooing 'it's okay, don't worry' — {dog_name} reads your worry, not your words.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} is already scared of the vet, ask about pre-visit calming medication and look for a Fear Free certified veterinarian in your area.

title: Car Ride Desensitization
overview: Teach {dog_name} to enjoy car rides instead of dreading them. Many puppies associate the car with the vet — let's fix that.
steps: Sit in the parked car with {dog_name}. Treats and play. Engine off. 5 minutes.
Next session: engine on, car parked. Treats.
Next: short drive around the block. Treat at destination.
Gradually increase trip length. Always end somewhere good (park, friend's house, drive-through).
Practice getting in and out calmly. Reward 'wait' before exiting.
success_criteria: {dog_name} jumps into the car willingly and rides calmly for 15+ minutes.
pro_tips: Use a crate or crash-tested harness — loose dogs in cars are a safety hazard.
Make 80% of car trips go somewhere fun. Only 20% should be vet/groomer.
common_mistakes: Only driving to the vet — of course {dog_name} hates the car.
Feeding a big meal before car rides — motion sickness is common in puppies.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} gets carsick, try the front seat (more stable), crack a window, and keep rides under 10 minutes while building tolerance. Your vet can recommend anti-nausea medication for longer trips.

title: Cafe and Restaurant Manners
overview: Train {dog_name} to settle calmly under a table at a pet-friendly cafe or restaurant. This is the ultimate settle exercise in a real-world environment.
steps: Bring {dog_name}'s 'place' mat to a quiet outdoor cafe.
Sit down. Place mat under the table. Ask {dog_name} to 'place.'
Give a stuffed Kong or long-lasting chew immediately.
Mark and treat any calm behavior: lying down, resting, chewing quietly.
Start with 15-minute visits and build to longer outings.
success_criteria: {dog_name} lies calmly under a cafe table for 20 minutes.
pro_tips: Go after exercise — a tired puppy is a calm puppy.
The portable mat is key — it tells {dog_name} 'this is your spot' in any environment.
common_mistakes: Bringing an under-exercised puppy to a cafe — recipe for disaster.
Choosing a busy location for the first attempt — start with a quiet patio.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} is too excited to settle, practice 'place' at home with increasing distractions first. The cafe is level 10 — make sure they've passed levels 1-9.

title: Visitors at the Door
overview: Transform door-bell chaos into a calm, structured greeting. This combines door manners, impulse control, and settle into one real-world scenario.
steps: Set up: {dog_name} on leash, baby gate between entrance and living area.
Visitor knocks. Ask {dog_name} for a sit. If they sit, mark and treat. If not, wait.
Open the door with {dog_name} behind the gate. Let them see the visitor.
When {dog_name} is calm (4 on the floor or sitting), open the gate.
Visitor enters calmly. If {dog_name} jumps, visitor turns away. Only calm behavior gets attention.
success_criteria: {dog_name} remains behind the gate calmly while the door is opened, then greets the visitor without jumping.
pro_tips: Practice with friends who will follow the rules. Don't test with people who'll ignore your protocol.
Keep a treat jar by the front door for quick reinforcement.
common_mistakes: Yelling at {dog_name} to stop barking — yelling is joining the party.
Letting excited visitors encourage jumping — brief your guests BEFORE they arrive.
troubleshooting: If the doorbell itself is the trigger, desensitize to the sound separately (play it at low volume during meals). Then address the visitor protocol.

title: Handling Distractions on Walks
overview: Build a toolkit of responses for common walk distractions: squirrels, cats, cyclists, joggers, and loud noises.
steps: Identify {dog_name}'s top 3 walk distractions (rank by intensity).
For each: find the threshold distance where {dog_name} notices but can still take treats.
Use "look at me" at threshold distance. Mark and treat for eye contact.
Gradually decrease distance over sessions (weeks, not days).
Add an "emergency U-turn": say "this way!" and jog in the opposite direction. Treat when they follow.
success_criteria: {dog_name} can pass their #1 distraction at 10 feet with attention on you.
pro_tips: The emergency U-turn is your best friend for unexpected distractions. Practice it daily.
Carry a squeaky toy as backup — some dogs respond better to toys than treats in high-arousal moments.
common_mistakes: Tightening the leash when you see a distraction coming — your tension travels down the leash.
Approaching distractions head-on — cross the street or angle away to create distance.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} is reactive (barking, lunging) at specific triggers, this exercise alone may not be enough. Reactivity benefits from a structured behavior modification plan with a certified trainer.

title: Dog-Friendly Store Visit
overview: Take {dog_name} to a pet-friendly store for a controlled socialization and training experience. New sights, sounds, smells, and people in one trip.
steps: Choose a quiet time (weekday morning, not Saturday afternoon).
Enter the store and let {dog_name} acclimate for 1-2 minutes near the entrance.
Walk slowly through aisles, rewarding calm behavior and eye contact.
Practice sit when someone wants to pet {dog_name}.
Keep the visit to 15 minutes maximum. Leave before {dog_name} gets overwhelmed.
success_criteria: {dog_name} walks through a pet store on a loose leash, greets 2 people politely, and leaves without a meltdown.
pro_tips: Pet stores are ideal because everyone there likes dogs. Built-in positive social experiences.
Let {dog_name} sniff products on low shelves — this is enrichment.
common_mistakes: Going during peak hours — too overwhelming for a puppy.
Letting every person and dog rush up to {dog_name} — control the interactions.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} shuts down in the store, find a quiet corner, sit on the floor, and just let them decompress with treats. No pressure to walk or interact.

title: Elevator Etiquette
overview: Teach {dog_name} to enter, ride, and exit elevators calmly. Essential for apartment living, vet visits, and multi-story buildings.
steps: Practice 'wait' at the elevator door before entering. Treat for patience.
Enter the elevator first, then invite {dog_name} in with 'let's go.'
Scatter treats on the floor the moment you enter to create positive association.
Practice sit-stay during the ride. Treat for calm behavior.
At your floor, cue 'wait' before the doors open. Exit first, then release {dog_name}.
If other people or dogs are in the elevator, keep {dog_name} in a sit between your legs.
success_criteria: {dog_name} rides an elevator calmly in a sit, waiting for cue to enter and exit.
pro_tips: If possible, practice during quiet hours when the elevator is likely empty. {breed_tip}
The elevator 'ding' and door sounds can be scary — treat every time they happen.
For small puppies, start by just entering a stationary elevator with open doors.
common_mistakes: Letting {dog_name} rush in when doors open — this is dangerous with other passengers.
Ignoring {dog_name}'s stress signals (panting, cowering) — keep rides short until they're confident.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} won't enter the elevator, practice with doors held open, treating just for stepping one paw in. Build from there.

title: Groomer Preparation
overview: Prepare {dog_name} for their first professional grooming experience. If they're already comfortable with handling, the groomer's job is easier and {dog_name} stays calm.
supplies: electric toothbrush or clippers (turned off, then on)
steps: Lift {dog_name} onto a stable table or counter with a non-slip mat. Treat for calm.
Turn on an electric toothbrush near (not touching) {dog_name}. Treat heavily for tolerance.
Gradually bring the vibrating device closer. Touch it to their body briefly. Treat.
Practice touching every part of {dog_name}'s body on the table: paws, ears, tail, belly, face.
Gently restrain {dog_name} in the positions a groomer would need: standing still, chin held gently, paw extended.
Visit a groomer for a 'puppy intro' — no grooming, just treats and positive association with the facility.
success_criteria: {dog_name} stands calmly on a table for 2 minutes while being handled all over, tolerating vibration nearby.
pro_tips: Ask your groomer if they do 'puppy intro visits' — most will happily do this for free. {breed_tip}
Start grooming prep early, even if {dog_name}'s breed doesn't need frequent grooming.
The table surface and height is often the scariest part. Practice at home first.
common_mistakes: First grooming visit being a full groom — too much too fast. Build up gradually.
Skipping the vibration desensitization — clippers are the #1 grooming fear for dogs.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} panics on a table, practice on a very low surface first (a step stool) and work up to table height. Always use a non-slip mat.

title: Travel Crate + Hotel Protocol
overview: Prepare {dog_name} for overnight stays away from home: hotels, Airbnbs, or visits to family. A dog who is crate-trained and travel-confident opens up your world.
steps: Practice crate time in different rooms of your house — the living room, a bedroom, the garage.
Take the crate to a friend's house. Let {dog_name} settle in the crate in a new environment. Treat heavily.
Practice the full 'hotel routine': crate, Kong, white noise machine/phone playing sounds, lights off.
When {dog_name} is comfortable at a friend's house, try a real overnight somewhere.
Bring familiar items: their blanket, a worn shirt of yours, their regular food.
Stick to {dog_name}'s normal routine as much as possible (feeding times, potty times, walk times).
success_criteria: {dog_name} settles in a crate in an unfamiliar environment within 15 minutes and sleeps through the night.
pro_tips: A tired puppy travels better. Exercise {dog_name} well before travel days. {breed_tip}
White noise covers unfamiliar sounds in hotels. Download a white noise app.
Call hotels ahead to confirm pet policy and any size/breed restrictions.
common_mistakes: First trip being a week-long vacation — start with one overnight close to home.
Abandoning the crate routine on vacation 'because we're on holiday' — consistency matters most in new environments.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} won't settle in a new place, try putting the crate in the bathroom (smaller, quieter, more den-like). Some dogs prefer the enclosed space.

title: Emergency Handling Protocol
overview: Teach {dog_name} to tolerate being grabbed, lifted, and handled urgently. In an emergency, you need to be able to pick up or restrain your dog without a fight.
steps: Practice gently grabbing {dog_name}'s collar. Mark and treat immediately.
Practice lifting {dog_name} briefly (1-2 seconds) and setting them back down. Treat.
Practice holding {dog_name} still (gentle bear hug for 3 seconds). Treat on release.
Practice grabbing a paw suddenly. Treat immediately.
Practice picking {dog_name} up from behind (reaching over). Treat on pickup.
Do 5-10 reps of each daily for a week. The goal: {dog_name} relaxes into any handling because it always predicts treats.
success_criteria: {dog_name} tolerates collar grabs, lifts, and brief restraint without panicking, biting, or struggling.
pro_tips: This is safety training. A dog who panics when grabbed can hurt themselves or you in an emergency. {breed_tip}
Practice with all family members — {dog_name} needs to accept handling from anyone.
Pair every grab with a treat. We want {dog_name} to think 'grabbed = treat party.'
common_mistakes: Only grabbing {dog_name} when something bad follows (bath, nail trim) — practice random happy grabs daily.
Grabbing too roughly in practice — always start gentle and build tolerance.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} growls or snaps when grabbed, this is a fear response. Start with very gentle touches (not grabs) and work with a certified trainer if the reaction is severe.

title: People Socialization Checklist
overview: Expose {dog_name} to many different types of people during the critical socialization window (8-16 weeks). Positive experiences now prevent fear and aggression later.
steps: Create a checklist: men, women, children, elderly, people with hats, sunglasses, beards, uniforms, wheelchairs, strollers.
Introduce one new type per day. Let {dog_name} approach at their own pace.
Have the person toss treats on the ground near {dog_name} (not hand-feed yet).
If {dog_name} approaches voluntarily, the person can offer a treat from their hand.
NEVER force an interaction. If {dog_name} hides or retreats, increase distance and try again later.
success_criteria: {dog_name} voluntarily approaches 10+ different types of people by 16 weeks of age.
pro_tips: Quality over quantity — one positive interaction beats ten overwhelming ones.
Carry treats everywhere during socialization weeks. Every outing is a training opportunity.
common_mistakes: Forcing {dog_name} to be held by strangers — let THEM choose to engage.
Only socializing with dog people — {dog_name} needs to meet people who are nervous around dogs too.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} is fearful of a specific type of person, don't flood them. Create distance, pair the scary person with high-value treats, and let {dog_name} observe from safety.

title: Sound Desensitization
overview: Help {dog_name} become comfortable with everyday sounds that might otherwise cause fear: doorbells, vacuum cleaners, sirens, thunder, fireworks.
steps: Find sound clips on YouTube: thunderstorms, fireworks, vacuum, doorbell, traffic.
Play the sound at the LOWEST possible volume while {dog_name} eats or plays.
If {dog_name} ignores the sound, great — treat and slowly increase volume over sessions.
If {dog_name} reacts (freezes, whines, hides), the volume is too high. Reduce it.
Work on one sound category at a time. 2-3 sessions per day, 5 minutes each.
success_criteria: {dog_name} remains calm (eating, playing, or resting) while sounds play at moderate volume.
pro_tips: Start this before storm season. Desensitization takes weeks to be reliable.
Play sounds during meal times — this pairs scary sounds with the best activity.
common_mistakes: Starting too loud — the puppy should barely notice the sound at first.
Comforting fearful behavior with baby talk — this can reinforce the fear. Stay calm and neutral instead.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} is already terrified of a specific sound (like thunder), consult your vet about anxiety management before attempting desensitization. They may need calming supplements or medication to make the process work.

title: Surface and Texture Exploration
overview: Expose {dog_name} to different surfaces underfoot. Puppies who only walk on carpet may fear metal grates, slippery floors, grass, or gravel later.
steps: Set up a mini obstacle course at home: a cookie sheet, bubble wrap, a towel, a plastic bag, a piece of cardboard.
Let {dog_name} investigate at their own pace. Treat for any approach or contact.
Don't lure them ONTO scary surfaces — let them choose to step on.
Once comfortable at home, seek out different surfaces outside: metal grates, manhole covers, rubber mats, wet grass, gravel, sand.
Mark and treat each new surface exploration.
success_criteria: {dog_name} walks across 5+ different surfaces without hesitation.
pro_tips: Wobbly surfaces (a board on a ball) build confidence and body awareness — great for agility foundations.
Take {dog_name} to pet-friendly hardware stores for exposure to hard floors and wide aisles.
common_mistakes: Placing {dog_name} ON a scary surface — they need to choose to step on it.
Laughing at their fear — stay supportive and positive.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} won't step on something, put treats along the edge and let them eat while standing next to it. Proximity without contact is still progress.

title: Handling and Body Awareness
overview: Teach {dog_name} to enjoy being touched everywhere on their body. This makes vet visits, grooming, and daily health checks stress-free.
steps: Start with areas {dog_name} already likes (chest, shoulders).
Touch briefly, mark 'yes!' and treat.
Gradually move to sensitive areas: paws, ears, mouth, belly, tail.
For each area: touch → treat, touch longer → treat, hold → treat, manipulate → treat.
Practice opening their mouth, looking in ears, holding each paw, lifting their tail.
success_criteria: {dog_name} allows you to touch all body parts including paws, ears, and mouth for 5 seconds without pulling away.
pro_tips: Do this daily during cuddle time. Make it a routine, not a special event.
Gently simulate vet handling: lift lips to see teeth, look in ears, press belly softly.
common_mistakes: Holding {dog_name} down when they resist — this creates handling aversion.
Skipping paw handling — nail trims will be a nightmare later if paws are off-limits.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} is paw-sensitive, start by just touching their shoulder, then elbow, then lower leg, then paw — inch toward the sensitive area over multiple sessions.

title: Visual Dog Socialization
overview: Before {dog_name} is fully vaccinated, they can still see other dogs from a distance. This builds positive associations without health risks.
steps: Sit outside a dog park, pet store, or on a bench along a walking path.
Every time {dog_name} notices another dog, mark 'yes!' and treat. Dog = treat.
Keep distance comfortable — {dog_name} should be curious, not overwhelmed.
If {dog_name} lunges or barks, you're too close. Move back.
3-4 sessions per week during the socialization window.
success_criteria: {dog_name} watches other dogs pass at 15 feet without barking, lunging, or freezing.
pro_tips: Pair this with puppy socialization classes (controlled environments with vaccinated puppies).
Carry {dog_name} in areas where unvaccinated pups shouldn't walk — they can still SEE the world.
common_mistakes: Taking unvaccinated puppies to dog parks — parvo risk is real.
Assuming {dog_name} needs to play with every dog they see — observation is socialization.
troubleshooting: If {dog_name} is overly excited or frustrated watching dogs, increase distance until they can eat treats calmly. Reward the calm watching, not the frantic pulling.

title: New Environment Exploration
overview: Take {dog_name} to new places regularly during their socialization period. Puppies who only know home and the vet become fearful of novelty.
steps: Make a list of 10 environments: pet store, outdoor cafe, park, friend's house, parking lot, school grounds (after hours), vet lobby (just to visit), car wash (from car), farmer's market, playground.
Visit 1-2 new places per week.
Let {dog_name} take in the sights, sounds, and smells at their own pace.
Mark and treat calm behavior. If they sniff something bravely, bonus treats.
Keep visits short (10-15 minutes) and end on a positive note.
success_criteria: {dog_name} enters 5+ new environments without showing signs of stress (tucked tail, whale eye, excessive panting).
pro_tips: Take treats to the vet for 'happy visits' — just walk in, get treats from the staff, and leave. No needles.
